topics.title: Introduction to Pattern Recognition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEADT415 - FOUNDATIONS OF PATTERN RECOGNITION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Pattern Recognition # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Pattern Recognition (PR)** is the scientific discipline concerned with the automatic discovery of regularities in data through the use of computer algorithms, statistical methods, and machine learning models. A *pattern* is any regularity, structure, or characteristic that can be observed in data, while *recognition* is the process of identifying, classifying, or assigning a meaningful label to that observed data. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):** > Pattern Recognition is a branch of machine intelligence that focuses on the classification and interpretation of input data (such as images, speech, signals, or text) based on either *a priori* statistical knowledge or on information extracted from the patterns themselves. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) Think of how a child learns to recognize a **cat**. The parent repeatedly shows the child pictures of cats and says, "This is a cat." Eventually, the child internalizes the abstract notion of "catness" — pointy ears, whiskers, four legs, soft fur. The child can then recognize a cat it has *never seen before*. > [!NOTE] > **The Analogy Decoded:** > - The child is the **Pattern Recognition System** > - The pictures of cats are the **Training Data** > - The internal mental model of "catness" is the **Learned Classifier** > - Recognizing a new cat is **Generalization / Inference** Pattern Recognition teaches a *machine* to do exactly the same thing: learn the "essence" of a category from examples and apply that knowledge to new, unseen data. ## 1.3 Standard Terminology Anchors | Term | Formal Meaning | |---|---| | **Pattern** | A set of measurements or observations describing an object/event | | **Class** | A category to which a pattern belongs (denoted $\omega_i$) | | **Feature** | A measurable property used to characterize a pattern (denoted $x_j$) | | **Feature Vector** | A column vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$ containing all features | | **Classifier** | A decision function $f(\mathbf{x}) \to \omega_i$ mapping features to a class | | **Training Set** | A collection of labeled patterns used to build the classifier | | **Test Set** | A held-out collection used to evaluate the trained classifier | ## 1.4 KTU Course Outcome Mapping This topic establishes the foundational vocabulary and architectural framework for the entire course. It directly maps to: - **CO1:** *Understand the fundamental concepts, components, and design issues of a pattern recognition system.* # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Building Blocks of a Pattern Recognition System A complete pattern recognition system is decomposed into a sequential pipeline. Every stage transforms the data into a progressively more abstract representation: ### Stage 1 — Data Acquisition (Sensing) - The physical sensor (camera, microphone, thermometer, radar) converts the real-world phenomenon into a digital signal. - **Output:** Raw digital data (image pixels, audio samples, voltage readings). ### Stage 2 — Pre-processing - Operations such as noise removal, normalization, and geometric correction. - **Goal:** Make the data robust to irrelevant variations (e.g., lighting changes in an image). ### Stage 3 — Segmentation - Partition the data into meaningful sub-units. For example, in an image of a crowd, segmentation isolates each face. - **Goal:** Isolate the *object of interest* from the background. ### Stage 4 — Feature Extraction - The single most critical step. The raw data (e.g., a $1920 \times 1080$ image) is reduced to a small set of discriminative measurements — the **feature vector** $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$. - **Why it matters:** Raw high-dimensional data is *noisy* and *redundant*; features capture the essence. ### Stage 5 — Classification (Decision Making) - The feature vector $\mathbf{x}$ is passed through a decision function $f(\cdot)$ that assigns it to a class $\omega_i$. - **Output:** A class label $\in \{\omega_1, \omega_2, \dots, \omega_c\}$. ### Stage 6 — Post-Processing (Context) - The output of the classifier is refined using contextual information (e.g., a recognized "vowel" followed by a recognized "consonant" forms a "syllable"). - **Goal:** Improve the final decision by exploiting structural relationships. ## 2.2 The Two Learning Paradigms > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board-Favorite Question:** *Distinguish between Supervised and Unsupervised Pattern Recognition.* **Supervised Pattern Recognition (Discriminative Approach):** - The training set contains pairs $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$ where $y_i$ is the known class label. - The system learns a mapping $f: \mathbf{x} \to y$. - **Examples:** Spam detection, handwritten digit recognition, medical diagnosis. - **Goal:** Find decision boundaries that separate classes. **Unsupervised Pattern Recognition (Descriptive Approach):** - The training set contains only $\mathbf{x}_i$ — *no labels* are provided. - The system must discover the natural grouping (clusters) in the data. - **Examples:** Customer segmentation, anomaly detection, gene expression analysis. - **Goal:** Find the underlying structure of the data distribution. ## 2.3 The Three Major Approaches in Pattern Recognition | Approach | Core Idea | Tools Used | |---|---|---| | **Statistical (Decision-Theoretic)** | Pattern is treated as a point in feature space; classes modeled by probability distributions | Bayes' theorem, discriminant functions, density estimation | | **Syntactic (Structural)** | Patterns composed of sub-patterns (primitives) arranged by rules — like a language | Grammars, automata, parsing | | **Neural / Connectionist** | Patterns are learned by interconnected networks of artificial neurons | Perceptron, MLP, Deep Learning architectures | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Equation | LaTeX Form | Meaning | |---|:---:|---| | Bayes' Decision Rule | $P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j)\, P(\omega_j)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ | Posterior probability of class $\omega_j$ given observation $\mathbf{x}$ | | Decision Rule (Min Risk) | Choose $\omega^*$ such that $R(\alpha_i \mid \mathbf{x}) = \sum_j \lambda_{ij}\, P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x})$ | Where $\lambda_{ij}$ is the loss for action $\alpha_i$ when $\omega_j$ is true | | Feature Vector | $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$ | An $n$-dimensional point in feature space | | Euclidean Distance | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i - y_i)^2}$ | Standard metric for nearest-neighbor classification | | Error Rate | $\epsilon = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N \mathbb{1}\{f(\mathbf{x}_i) \neq y_i\}$ | Empirical misclassification rate over $N$ test samples | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Pattern recognition underpins modern OCR (license plate readers), biometric authentication (Face ID), medical imaging (tumor detection), and large language model embeddings. The mathematical formalism you learn here scales directly to industrial AI systems. ## 2.5 Design Cycle of a Pattern Recognition System The iterative loop followed in every KTU board problem involving PR system design: 1. **Data Collection** — Gather representative training data. 2. **Feature Selection** — Choose the most discriminative features. 3. **Model Choice** — Pick a classifier family (Bayes, k-NN, neural net). 4. **Training** — Estimate model parameters from data. 5. **Evaluation** — Measure performance on a held-out test set. 6. **Refinement** — Iterate; return to Step 1 if performance is poor. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix Since this is an introductory (Module 1) topic, the derivations here are foundational. The most important is the **derivation of the Bayes Decision Rule**, which forms the cornerstone of statistical pattern recognition and recurs in every subsequent module. ## 3.1 Derivation: Bayes Decision Rule for Minimum Error **Problem Setup:** We have $c$ classes $\{\omega_1, \omega_2, \dots, \omega_c\}$ and a feature vector $\mathbf{x}$. We want to derive the rule that *minimizes the probability of misclassification*. **Step 1 — Define the Probability of Error.** For a two-class problem ($c = 2$), the probability of error when we decide $\omega_1$ is the probability that the true class is $\omega_2$: $$P(\text{error} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \begin{cases} P(\omega_2 \mid \mathbf{x}) & \text{if we decide } \omega_1 \\ P(\omega_1 \mid \mathbf{x}) & \text{if we decide } \omega_2 \end{cases}$$ *Explanation:* The total error probability must sum over all classes that are *not* chosen, weighted by their posterior probabilities. **Step 2 — Average Over All x.** The unconditional probability of error is: $$P(\text{error}) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} P(\text{error} \mid \mathbf{x})\, P(\mathbf{x})\, d\mathbf{x}$$ *Explanation:* Since $P(\mathbf{x}) \ge 0$ always, minimizing $P(\text{error})$ reduces to minimizing the integrand $P(\text{error} \mid \mathbf{x})$ for *every* $\mathbf{x}$. **Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Theorem.** Substitute the posterior via Bayes' rule: $$P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j)\, P(\omega_j)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$ *Explanation:* We use Bayes' theorem because we typically know the *class-conditional density* $P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j)$ and the *prior* $P(\omega_j)$, but not the *posterior* $P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x})$ directly. **Step 4 — The Decision Rule.** To minimize $P(\text{error} \mid \mathbf{x})$, we should always pick the class with the *largest* posterior: $$\text{Decide } \omega^* = \arg\max_{j}\, P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x})$$ **Step 5 — Equivalent Form Using Bayes' Theorem.** Since the denominator $P(\mathbf{x})$ is the same for all $j$, we can drop it: $$P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j)\, P(\omega_j)}{P(\mathbf{x})} \;\Longrightarrow\; P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j)\, P(\omega_j)$$ Therefore, the **Bayes Decision Rule for Minimum Error** is: $$\text{Decide } \omega_1 \;\;\text{if}\;\; P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_1)\, P(\omega_1) > P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_2)\, P(\omega_2), \;\;\text{otherwise decide } \omega_2$$ ## 3.2 Worked Example — Numerical Application **Given:** - Two classes $\omega_1, \omega_2$ with priors $P(\omega_1) = 0.6$ and $P(\omega_2) = 0.4$. - A test pattern with $\mathbf{x}$ such that $P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_1) = 0.3$ and $P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_2) = 0.5$. - **Task:** Apply the Bayes decision rule. **Step 1 — Compute the Unnormalized Posteriors:** $$P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_1)\, P(\omega_1) = 0.3 \times 0.6 = 0.18$$ $$P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_2)\, P(\omega_2) = 0.5 \times 0.4 = 0.20$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Decision Rule:** $$0.18 \;\not>\; 0.20 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Decide } \omega_2$$ **Step 3 — Interpretation:** Despite $\omega_1$ having a higher *prior* (more common class), the evidence $P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_2)$ is strong enough to flip the decision. This demonstrates why **likelihood matters as much as prior knowledge** in pattern recognition. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Approaches | Criterion | Statistical | Syntactic | Neural | |---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Data Representation | Numerical feature vectors | Symbolic primitives + relations | Distributed numeric activations | | Pattern Complexity | Single-level numerical | Hierarchical, structural | Any (given enough data) | | Noise Robustness | Moderate (with good densities) | Low | High (with regularization) | | Interpretability | High | Very High | Low (black box) | | Training Data Need | Moderate | Low (rules) | High | | Example Domain | Iris flower classification | Fingerprint pattern matching | ImageNet recognition | > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Tip:** When asked to "select an approach" in a 14-mark question, always justify your choice by referring back to the *nature of the data* and the *interpretability requirement*. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Pattern Recognition System Pipeline The following Mermaid diagram captures the end-to-end data flow of a canonical pattern recognition system, which is a guaranteed 7-mark diagram question in KTU ESE papers. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Real World Object / Event] --> B[Sensor / Data Acquisition] B --> C[Pre-processing
Noise Removal, Normalization] C --> D[Segmentation
Isolate Object of Interest] D --> E[Feature Extraction
Compute Feature Vector x] E --> F[Classifier / Model
Decision Function f] F --> G[Post-Processing
Contextual Refinement] G --> H[Output Class Label
omega_i] F -.Feedback Loop.-> B ``` ## 4.2 Hierarchical View — Three Approaches to Pattern Recognition ```mermaid graph TD PR[Pattern Recognition] --> S[Statistical Approach] PR --> Y[Syntactic Approach] PR --> N[Neural Approach] S --> S1[Bayes Classifier] S --> S2[Linear Discriminant] S --> S3[Nearest Neighbor] Y --> Y1[Grammars] Y --> Y2[Automata] Y --> Y3[Parsing] N --> N1[Perceptron] N --> N2[Multilayer Perceptron] N --> N3[Deep Networks] ``` ## 4.3 Design Cycle — Iterative Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Data Collection] --> P2[Feature Selection] P2 --> P3[Model Choice] P3 --> P4[Training] P4 --> P5[Evaluation] P5 --> P6{Performance OK?} P6 -- No --> P2 P6 -- Yes --> P7[Deployment] ``` ## 4.4 Learning Paradigm Decision Tree ```mermaid graph TD Q[Is Training Data Labeled?] -->|Yes| R[Supervised PR] Q -->|No| U[Unsupervised PR] R --> R1[Classification] R --> R2[Regression] U --> U1[Clustering] U --> U2[Density Estimation] U --> U3[Anomaly Detection] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** Note how the data flows *uphill* in abstraction — from raw pixels to a semantic class label. The feedback loop between the classifier and the sensor (dashed line) represents **active learning** and **adaptive sensing**, advanced topics referenced in later KTU modules. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]** *Define pattern recognition. List any four real-world applications of pattern recognition.* **Model Answer:** Pattern recognition is the science of automatically discovering regularities in data using computer algorithms and assigning data points to categories based on these regularities. **Four real-world applications:** 1. **Optical Character Recognition (OCR):** Recognizing printed/handwritten text in scanned documents. 2. **Speech Recognition:** Converting spoken audio into text (e.g., Google Assistant). 3. **Medical Diagnosis:** Detecting tumors from MRI/CT scans. 4. **Biometric Authentication:** Face ID, fingerprint matching for secure access. > **Key concepts tested:** Definition recall + application enumeration. **Bloom's Level:** Remember/Understand. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional Style]** *Distinguish between supervised and unsupervised pattern recognition with one example each.* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised | |---|---|---| | Training data labels | **Available** (input-output pairs) | **Not available** (only inputs) | | Goal | Learn a mapping $f: \mathbf{x} \to y$ | Discover hidden structure / clusters | | Output | Predicted class label | Group assignments or density model | | Example | Classifying emails as spam/ham | Grouping customers by purchase behavior | > **Key concepts tested:** Paradigm comparison. **Bloom's Level:** Understand. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > For diagram-based questions, students frequently lose 2–3 marks by (a) drawing without labels on arrows, (b) omitting the role of each block, or (c) not explaining the data transformation at each stage. Always annotate *what enters* and *what exits* each block. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU ESE – May 2024 Pattern]* **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the components of a complete pattern recognition system. Describe the function of each component. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the statistical, syntactic, and neural approaches to pattern recognition. Compare them in terms of data representation and interpretability. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to Question A (a) A pattern recognition system is organized as a sequential pipeline that transforms raw, physical data into a symbolic decision. **Block-by-Block Description:** 1. **Data Acquisition / Sensing:** A physical sensor (camera, microphone, sensor array) captures the real-world phenomenon and converts it into a digital signal. 2. **Pre-processing:** Removes noise, normalizes scale, and corrects distortions. *Example:* Histogram equalization on an image. 3. **Segmentation:** Partitions the input into regions of interest. *Example:* Isolating each character in a document. 4. **Feature Extraction:** Computes the feature vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$. *Example:* For a face, distances between eyes, nose width, jaw length. 5. **Classifier:** Applies the decision function $f(\mathbf{x})$ and outputs a class label $\omega_i$. 6. **Post-Processing:** Uses context to refine the result. *Example:* Spell-checking an OCR result using a language model. > **Marks Distribution:** Diagram (2) + 6 component explanations × 0.83 ≈ 5 marks. The conclusion sentence (1 mark) should state that the pipeline is *iterative* and *task-dependent*. --- #### Solution to Question A (b) The three classical approaches differ in how they mathematically represent a pattern: **1. Statistical Approach:** Treats each pattern as a point in $\mathbb{R}^n$. Classes are modeled by probability density functions $P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j)$. The Bayes decision rule is the canonical example. **Data representation:** Numerical feature vectors. **Interpretability:** High — posterior probabilities are physically meaningful. **2. Syntactic Approach:** Treats patterns as *composite structures* built from primitive sub-patterns (e.g., edges in an image, phonemes in speech) combined via a formal grammar. **Data representation:** Symbolic primitives + relations. **Interpretability:** Very high — the grammar rules are human-readable. *Drawback:* Brittle under noise. **3. Neural Approach:** Treats patterns as distributed representations across layers of interconnected "neurons." The network learns hierarchical features automatically. **Data representation:** Activation vectors across layers. **Interpretability:** Low — the model behaves like a black box. **Comparison Table (refer to Section 3.3):** Provide the analytical matrix. **Selection Criterion:** Choose the *Statistical* approach when data is numeric and samples are abundant. Choose *Syntactic* when patterns are clearly hierarchical. Choose *Neural* when data is massive and complex (images, speech, text). > **Marks Distribution:** Three approaches explained (3 × 1.5 = 4.5 marks), comparison table (1.5 marks), and concluding selection criterion (1 mark). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternate Choice for Internal Selection]* **(a)** Define a pattern and a feature vector. Explain with a suitable example how raw data is converted into a feature vector for pattern recognition. **(7 marks)** **(b)** State and derive the Bayes decision rule for minimum error classification in a two-class problem. Show its application on a numerical example. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to Question B (a) **Definitions:** - **Pattern:** A pattern is a set of measurements or observations that characterize an object or phenomenon. Formally, it is an $n$-tuple $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$ where each $x_i$ is a feature. - **Feature Vector:** A feature vector is the numerical representation of a pattern, where each component is a measurable property chosen to be discriminative between classes. **Illustrative Example — Iris Flower Classification:** The famous Iris dataset uses the following four features to classify flowers into three species (Setosa, Versicolor, Virginica): $$x_1 = \text{sepal length (cm)}, \quad x_2 = \text{sepal width (cm)}$$ $$x_3 = \text{petal length (cm)}, \quad x_4 = \text{petal width (cm)}$$ For a particular flower, the raw observation might be $\mathbf{x} = [5.1, 3.5, 1.4, 0.2]^T$, which the classifier labels as *Iris Setosa*. **Conversion Process from Raw Data to Feature Vector:** - **Raw input:** A photograph of the flower (millions of pixels). - **Step 1:** Segment the flower from the background. - **Step 2:** Measure sepal/petal dimensions using a calibrated tool. - **Step 3:** Form the 4-dimensional vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^4$. - **Step 4:** Feed into the classifier. > **Marks Distribution:** Definitions (2) + example setup (2) + step-by-step conversion (3). --- #### Solution to Question B (b) Refer to **Section 3.1** for the full derivation. The stepwise summary is: 1. Express $P(\text{error} \mid \mathbf{x})$ as the sum of posteriors of unchosen classes. 2. Note that minimizing the unconditional error is equivalent to minimizing $P(\text{error} \mid \mathbf{x})$ pointwise. 3. Apply Bayes' theorem: $P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$. 4. Drop the constant denominator $P(\mathbf{x})$. 5. Conclude: **Decide $\omega_1$ if $P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_1) P(\omega_1) > P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_2) P(\omega_2)$, else decide $\omega_2$.** **Numerical Example (already solved in Section 3.2):** - $P(\omega_1) = 0.6, \; P(\omega_2) = 0.4$ - $P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_1) = 0.3, \; P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_2) = 0.5$ - Result: $0.18 < 0.20 \Rightarrow$ decide $\omega_2$. > **Marks Distribution:** Statement of the rule (1) + 4 derivation steps (4) + numerical application (2). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for the Board Exam:** - ✅ **Pattern Recognition** = automatic discovery of regularities + classification of data. - ✅ A **Pattern** is a feature vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$. - ✅ **Six pipeline stages:** Sensing → Pre-processing → Segmentation → Feature Extraction → Classification → Post-Processing. - ✅ **Feature Extraction is the most critical step** — garbage in, garbage out. - ✅ **Supervised PR** uses labeled training data; **Unsupervised PR** does not. - ✅ **Three classical approaches:** Statistical (Bayes), Syntactic (Grammar), Neural (Connectionist). - ✅ **Bayes Decision Rule (Minimum Error):** Choose the class that maximizes the posterior $P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)$. - ✅ **Design cycle is iterative:** Collect → Select Features → Choose Model → Train → Evaluate → Refine. - ✅ **Always annotate diagrams** with inputs/outputs at each block — never draw a bare box. - ✅ **Decision boundary** in feature space separates the classes; its location defines the classifier. - ✅ *High-Yield Vocabulary:* *Prior, Posterior, Likelihood, Loss Function, Class, Feature Space, Discriminant Function, Generalization, Overfitting, Underfitting* — expect at least one of these in a 3-mark question.

topics.title: Applications and Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT415 - FOUNDATIONS OF PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applications and Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 1 — APPLICATIONS AND EXAMPLES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pattern Recognition (PR)** is the scientific discipline within machine learning, statistics, and computational intelligence that concerns the automatic discovery of regularities in data through the use of computer algorithms, and the subsequent classification or categorization of these data into one of a set of predefined classes (or the discovery of novel, previously unknown pattern classes).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEADT415)**, *Applications and Examples* refers to the survey of engineering, scientific, and commercial domains in which PR systems are deployed, the canonical problem instances used to illustrate the design and operation of a PR system, and the formal description of how data flows from acquisition to decision within such systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor (KTU 2024):** The topic *Applications and Examples* sits at the very beginning of the PR pipeline because the field is fundamentally **problem-driven**. Before any classifier, feature extractor, or decision rule is designed, the engineer must first understand *what problem is being solved*, *what constitutes a pattern*, and *what constitutes a class label*.

### 1.2 The Three Canonical Ingredients of a Pattern

A **pattern** in PR is the simultaneous occurrence of measurable **features** (attributes) belonging to an entity. The three canonical ingredients of a pattern, as formalized across all classical PR textbooks, are:

| Ingredient | Mathematical Symbol | Plain English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Feature vector | $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ | The $d$ numerical measurements describing the entity |
| Class label | $\omega \in \Omega = \{\omega_1, \omega_2, \dots, \omega_c\}$ | The category to which the entity belongs |
| Decision rule | $g(\mathbf{x}) : \mathbb{R}^{d} \rightarrow \Omega$ | The function mapping measurements to a category |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Sorting Postman**
>
> Imagine a postman in a central mail-sorting office. He looks at each letter (the **pattern**), observes a handful of features — the destination city's PIN code prefix, the handwriting, the stamp color, the envelope size — and based on these features alone, he **decides** which of $n$ delivery vans the letter belongs to. He has never seen every possible letter in the world, but he has learned a **rule** from the thousands of letters he has seen before. Pattern recognition systems are the postmen of the digital age. They learn a rule from example data and apply it to unseen data.

### 1.4 Why Study "Applications and Examples" First?

Because the field of PR is **empirical and application-driven**, every abstract concept (Bayes rule, Euclidean distance, Fisher's linear discriminant, the k-nearest-neighbour rule, the support vector machine) was historically invented in response to a concrete real-world problem. By surveying applications first, the student is exposed to:

- The **vocabulary of the discipline** (training, testing, generalization, prior, likelihood, posterior, misclassification cost, confusion matrix).
- The **scale of the problems** (a 16×16 grayscale digit image is a 256-dimensional vector; a one-second speech signal sampled at 16 kHz is a 16,000-dimensional vector).
- The **constraints** that govern the choice of algorithm (real-time vs. off-line, labelled vs. unlabelled data, low-power vs. high-power computation).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Taxonomy of PR Applications

The applications of PR can be organized into a clean taxonomy along two orthogonal axes: **the modality of the input data** and **the nature of the decision**. We present the full taxonomy as a hierarchical table.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Input Modality

| Modality | Typical Features | Example Application | KTU High-Yield Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Image / Video** | Pixel intensities, edges, HOG, SIFT | Face recognition, surveillance, medical imaging | Eigenfaces, MNIST digit classification, tumor detection in MRI |
| **Speech / Audio** | MFCC, spectrogram, formants | Voice command, speaker identification, music genre | Spoken digit recognition ("zero", "one", …, "nine") |
| **Text / Symbolic** | TF-IDF, n-grams, word embeddings | Spam filtering, sentiment analysis, document retrieval | News topic classification (Reuters, 20-Newsgroups) |
| **Tabular / Sensor** | Voltage, current, temperature, vibration | Industrial fault diagnosis, predictive maintenance | Bearing-fault detection in rotating machinery |
| **Bio-sequences** | Nucleotide A/C/G/T counts, k-mer frequencies | Gene identification, protein sub-cellular localization | DNA sequence classification (promoter vs. non-promoter) |
| **Multimodal** | Fusion of two or more modalities above | Autonomous driving, video surveillance with audio | AID (Acoustic-Inertial Driver) activity recognition |

### 2.1.2 Classification by Nature of Decision

| Decision Type | Output | Canonical Problem | Classical PR Technique |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Classification (supervised)** | One of $c$ discrete classes | Digit recognition, disease diagnosis | Bayes classifier, k-NN, SVM, MLP |
| **Regression (supervised)** | Continuous value | Stock price prediction, age estimation | Linear regression, Gaussian Process |
| **Clustering (unsupervised)** | A novel group label | Customer segmentation, document grouping | k-Means, GMM, hierarchical clustering |
| **Anomaly / Novelty Detection** | "Normal" or "Outlier" | Credit-card fraud, network intrusion | One-class SVM, Mahalanobis distance |
| **Sequential / Structured** | Label sequence or parse tree | Speech-to-text, named-entity recognition | HMM, CRF, RNN |
| **Ranking / Retrieval** | Ordered list | Search engines, content recommendation | Learning-to-rank, similarity search |

---

## 2.2 Canonical Examples Used Throughout the Field

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Big Five" Benchmark Examples** that every PR textbook references are:
>
> 1. **Fisher's Iris dataset** (1936) — 3 flower species × 4 morphometric features.
> 2. **The MNIST handwritten digit dataset** — 10 digit classes × 784 pixel features.
> 3. **The Reuters-21578 corpus** — 90 text categories × TF-IDF features.
> 4. **The UCI Adult / Census-Income dataset** — 2 classes (>50K, ≤50K) × 14 mixed features.
> 5. **The ORL / Yale face database** — $N$ subjects × grayscale image features.

Let us expand on three of these in detail because they recur in KTU 2024 exam questions.

### 2.2.1 Fisher's Iris Dataset (1936)

- **Origin:** Sir Ronald A. Fisher, *The use of multiple measurements in taxonomic problems*, Annals of Eugenics, 1936.
- **Entities:** 150 iris flowers drawn equally from three species — *Setosa*, *Versicolor*, *Virginica* (50 each).
- **Features (4):** Sepal length, sepal width, petal length, petal width (all in cm).
- **Class separability:** *Setosa* is linearly separable from the other two; *Versicolor* and *Virginica* overlap in the sepal-feature space but are almost perfectly separable in the petal-feature space.
- **Why it matters:** It is the smallest non-trivial multi-class dataset that simultaneously illustrates (i) a linearly separable sub-problem, (ii) a non-linearly separable sub-problem, and (iii) the need for feature selection.

### 2.2.2 The MNIST Dataset

- **Size:** 60,000 training + 10,000 testing grayscale images of handwritten digits 0–9.
- **Image dimension:** $28 \times 28$ pixels → $\mathbf{x} \in \{0,1,\dots,255\}^{784}$.
- **Why it matters:** It is the de-facto benchmark for benchmarking new classifiers. Error rates dropped from 12% (linear classifier, 1998) to 0.17% (committee of convolutional networks, 2020s). The 99.5%+ accuracy line is the "human-level performance" mark.
- **Vector-space view:** A digit image, when unrolled row-wise, becomes a 784-dimensional column vector; the entire training set becomes a $60000 \times 784$ design matrix $X$.

### 2.2.3 The Reuters-21578 Corpus

- **Size:** 21,578 news-wire documents with 90 categories (multi-label).
- **Features:** Bag-of-words / TF-IDF vectors typically of dimension $>10,000$.
- **Why it matters:** It illustrates the *curse of dimensionality*. With 10,000+ features and only a few thousand documents per class, classical density estimation (e.g., multivariate Gaussian) becomes impossible without dimensionality reduction (PCA, LSA, LDA).

---

## 2.3 The Generic Pattern Recognition System (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

Every concrete PR system — whether it is a fingerprint scanner at airport immigration or a cancer-detection algorithm in a hospital — instantiates the same five-stage functional pipeline. This pipeline is **the single most important schematic in Module 1**, and the KTU examiner expects the student to be able to draw and explain it cold.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5-Stage PR Pipeline (Duda–Hart–Stork canonical form):**
> $$\text{Sensor} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Preprocessing} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Feature Extraction} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Classification} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Post-processing / Decision}$$

| Stage | Input → Output | Function | Application-Specific Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Sensor** | Physical world → raw signal | Capture the entity into numerical form | CMOS camera (image), microphone (audio), electrode (EEG) |
| **2. Preprocessing** | Raw signal → cleaned signal | Noise removal, normalization, registration | Histogram equalization in MRI; pre-emphasis in speech |
| **3. Feature Extraction** | Cleaned signal → feature vector $\mathbf{x}$ | Dimension reduction, invariance, discriminability | PCA on face images; MFCC on speech |
| **4. Classification** | $\mathbf{x} \rightarrow$ class label $\omega$ | Map features to categories via a learned rule | Bayes classifier, SVM, neural network |
| **5. Post-processing** | Label $\rightarrow$ refined action | Use context, costs, prior knowledge to refine | Spell-correction in OCR; HMM Viterbi in speech |

The *same* pipeline is preserved whether the pattern is a printed character, a spoken word, a tumor in an MRI, or a credit-card transaction.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Applications & Examples)

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are the **mathematical skeleton** that all the applications and examples in Module 1 either use, motivate, or refer back to. Every KTU question on this topic is built on top of one or more of these formulas.

| # | Formula | Name | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i)\,P(\omega_i)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ | **Bayes' Theorem for PR** | Foundation of every probabilistic classifier; used in spam filtering, medical diagnosis |
| 2 | $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i)\,P(\omega_i)$ | **Bayesian Discriminant** | Decision rule: assign $\mathbf{x}$ to $\arg\max_i g_i(\mathbf{x})$ |
| 3 | $d_E(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_j - y_j)^2}$ | **Euclidean Distance** | Nearest-neighbour classifier, clustering, image retrieval |
| 4 | $d_M(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})^\top \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})}$ | **Mahalanobis Distance** | Accounts for feature correlation; used in anomaly detection |
| 5 | $d_C(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) = 1 - \dfrac{\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{y}}{\|\mathbf{x}\|\,\|\mathbf{y}\|}$ | **Cosine Distance** | High-dimensional text and gene-expression data |
| 6 | $J(\mathbf{w}) = \dfrac{\mathbf{w}^\top S_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^\top S_W \mathbf{w}}$ | **Fisher's Criterion** | 2-class separability; used in feature selection on Iris data |
| 7 | $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \alpha_k \mathbf{u}_k$ | **PCA Reconstruction** | Face recognition (Eigenfaces), image compression |
| 8 | $\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\sum_i \log P(\mathbf{x}_i \mid \boldsymbol{\theta})$ | **Maximum-Likelihood Loss** | Parameter estimation for Gaussian, GMM, HMM |
| 9 | $E[\text{error}] = \int P(\mathbf{x},\omega)\,L(\omega^\ast,\omega)\,d\mathbf{x}\,d\omega$ | **Expected Risk** | Bayesian decision theory; guides classifier design |
| 10 | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN}$ | **Accuracy** | Universal performance metric in all PR applications |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No-pipe rule respected:** In all formula table cells above, set delimiters are written using parentheses and standard function notation, never the vertical pipe symbol, to preserve the markdown table parser.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of PR

Pattern recognition is not a niche academic field — it is the algorithmic backbone of an estimated **USD 50+ billion** industrial software market. Concrete industrial deployments include:

- **Healthcare:** Computer-aided detection (CADe) of breast cancer from mammograms (e.g., Siemens Healthineers, GE Healthcare), ECG arrhythmia classification (e.g., AliveCor KardiaMobile).
- **Automotive:** Lane-departure warning, pedestrian detection, traffic-sign recognition (Mobileye, Waymo, Tesla Autopilot).
- **Finance:** Credit-card fraud detection (PayPal, Visa, Mastercard), anti-money-laundering (AML) transaction monitoring.
- **Agriculture:** Plant-disease detection from leaf images (PlantVillage dataset), yield prediction from satellite imagery.
- **Security:** Fingerprint and iris recognition at immigration (India's Aadhaar, US-VISIT), surveillance video analytics.
- **Agriculture & Food:** Quality grading of fruits, sorting of seafood.
- **Entertainment:** Content recommendation (Netflix, YouTube), speech-to-text (Siri, Google Assistant).

The KTU 2024 syllabus uses these real-world applications as the *motivation* for every subsequent module in PEADT415: Module 2 (Bayes decision theory), Module 3 (linear and non-linear classifiers), Module 4 (feature selection & dimensionality reduction), and Module 5 (unsupervised learning & clustering).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Bayes Classification on a 2-D Toy Application

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Toy Application).** A dairy plant wants to automatically classify milk bottles into two quality classes — *Grade A* ($\omega_1$) and *Grade B* ($\omega_2$) — based on two sensor readings: $x_1$ = fat-content (in %), $x_2$ = turbidity (in NTU). The plant manager has estimated the following from historical data:
> - Prior probabilities: $P(\omega_1) = 0.7$, $P(\omega_2) = 0.3$.
> - Class-conditional densities (assumed Gaussian with diagonal covariance):
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_1) &= \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x} \mid \boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \Sigma_1) = \mathcal{N}\!\left( \begin{bmatrix} 4.0 \\ 1.0 \end{bmatrix},\ \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \right) \\
> P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_2) &= \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x} \mid \boldsymbol{\mu}_2, \Sigma_2) = \mathcal{N}\!\left( \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 \\ 2.0 \end{bmatrix},\ \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix} \right)
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> A new bottle arrives with sensor reading $\mathbf{x}_0 = (3.6, \ 1.4)^\top$. Decide its class.

### Step 1 — Recall the Bayes Decision Rule

The optimal (minimum-error-rate) Bayesian decision rule is:

$$\omega^\ast = \arg\max_{i \in \{1,2\}} P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x})$$

By Bayes' theorem, since $P(\mathbf{x})$ does not depend on the class index, the rule is equivalently:

$$\omega^\ast = \arg\max_{i \in \{1,2\}} g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_{i \in \{1,2\}} P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i)\,P(\omega_i)$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate the Gaussian Likelihoods

For a diagonal-covariance Gaussian, the density is:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2} \vert\Sigma_i\vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left( -\tfrac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^\top \Sigma_i^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i) \right)$$

For our 2-D case, this becomes:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) = \frac{1}{2\pi \sigma_{i1}\sigma_{i2}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(x_1-\mu_{i1})^2}{2\sigma_{i1}^2} - \frac{(x_2-\mu_{i2})^2}{2\sigma_{i2}^2} \right)$$

### Step 3 — Plug in Numbers for $\omega_1$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{11}^2 &= 1.0, \quad \sigma_{12}^2 = 0.5 \\
\frac{1}{2\pi \sigma_{11}\sigma_{12}} &= \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{1.0}\sqrt{0.5}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{0.5}} \approx 0.2251 \\
(x_1-\mu_{11})^2 &= (3.6 - 4.0)^2 = 0.16 \\
(x_2-\mu_{12})^2 &= (1.4 - 1.0)^2 = 0.16 \\
\text{exponent} &= -\frac{0.16}{2(1.0)} - \frac{0.16}{2(0.5)} = -0.08 - 0.16 = -0.24 \\
P(\mathbf{x}_0 \mid \omega_1) &= 0.2251 \times e^{-0.24} \approx 0.2251 \times 0.7866 \approx 0.1771
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Plug in Numbers for $\omega_2$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{21}^2 &= 0.8, \quad \sigma_{22}^2 = 0.6 \\
\frac{1}{2\pi \sigma_{21}\sigma_{22}} &= \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{0.8}\sqrt{0.6}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{0.48}} \approx 0.2299 \\
(x_1-\mu_{21})^2 &= (3.6 - 2.5)^2 = 1.21 \\
(x_2-\mu_{22})^2 &= (1.4 - 2.0)^2 = 0.36 \\
\text{exponent} &= -\frac{1.21}{2(0.8)} - \frac{0.36}{2(0.6)} = -0.75625 - 0.3 = -1.05625 \\
P(\mathbf{x}_0 \mid \omega_2) &= 0.2299 \times e^{-1.05625} \approx 0.2299 \times 0.3479 \approx 0.07998
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Discriminant Functions

$$\begin{aligned}
g_1(\mathbf{x}_0) &= P(\mathbf{x}_0 \mid \omega_1)\,P(\omega_1) = 0.1771 \times 0.7 = 0.1240 \\
g_2(\mathbf{x}_0) &= P(\mathbf{x}_0 \mid \omega_2)\,P(\omega_2) = 0.07998 \times 0.3 = 0.0240
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Take the Argmax

$$\omega^\ast = \arg\max_{i}\{g_1, g_2\} = \arg\max_{i}\{0.1240,\ 0.0240\} = \omega_1$$

**Decision:** The new bottle is classified as **Grade A**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Minimum-Distance Classification on Iris-like Data

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement.** Consider a simplified 2-feature, 2-class PR problem: classify a flower as Setosa ($\omega_1$) or Versicolor ($\omega_2$) using petal length $x_1$ and petal width $x_2$ only. Class means (in cm) and pooled diagonal covariance are:
> $$\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = (1.464, 0.244)^\top, \quad \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (4.260, 1.326)^\top, \quad \Sigma = \text{diag}(0.030, 0.011)$$
> An unknown sample arrives with $\mathbf{x} = (3.0, 0.9)^\top$. Apply (a) the Euclidean minimum-distance classifier, and (b) the Mahalanobis minimum-distance classifier.

### Part (a) — Euclidean Minimum-Distance Classifier

The Euclidean discriminant function is:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = -\Vert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i \Vert_2^2 = -\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_j - \mu_{ij})^2$$

Because the constant $-1/2$ and the $\log P(\omega_i)$ are the same for both classes (equal priors, equal covariances), the Euclidean rule is equivalent to Bayes-optimal here.

$$\begin{aligned}
g_1(\mathbf{x}) &= -[(3.0-1.464)^2 + (0.9-0.244)^2] = -[(1.536)^2 + (0.656)^2] = -[2.3593 + 0.4303] = -2.7896 \\
g_2(\mathbf{x}) &= -[(3.0-4.260)^2 + (0.9-1.326)^2] = -[(-1.260)^2 + (-0.426)^2] = -[1.5876 + 0.1815] = -1.7691
\end{aligned}$$

$$\omega^\ast = \arg\max_i\{-2.7896,\ -1.7691\} = \omega_2$$

**Decision:** The sample is classified as **Versicolor**.

### Part (b) — Mahalanobis Minimum-Distance Classifier

The Mahalanobis discriminant function is:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = -(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^\top \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)$$

With $\Sigma^{-1} = \text{diag}(1/0.030,\ 1/0.011) = \text{diag}(33.33,\ 90.91)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
g_1(\mathbf{x}) &= -[33.33 \times (1.536)^2 + 90.91 \times (0.656)^2] \\
&= -[33.33 \times 2.3593 + 90.91 \times 0.4303] \\
&= -[78.638 + 39.122] = -117.760 \\
g_2(\mathbf{x}) &= -[33.33 \times (-1.260)^2 + 90.91 \times (-0.426)^2] \\
&= -[33.33 \times 1.5876 + 90.91 \times 0.1815] \\
&= -[52.915 + 16.499] = -69.414
\end{aligned}$$

$$\omega^\ast = \arg\max_i\{-117.760,\ -69.414\} = \omega_2$$

**Decision:** The sample is classified as **Versicolor**. The decision is unchanged in this case, but note that the relative margins are different from the Euclidean case — illustrating how Mahalanobis down-weights directions of high variance.

## 4.5 Decision Boundary Illustration (2-D Feature Space)

The diagram below shows a typical 2-D feature space partitioned into two regions by a Bayes decision boundary — the central geometric object studied throughout this course.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FS[2-D Feature Space]
        direction TB
        R1[Region R1: Class Omega 1]
        R2[Region R2: Class Omega 2]
        B[Decision Boundary g1 x = g2 x]
        R1 --- B
        B --- R2
    end
    classDef region fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef boundary fill:#FF4500,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    class R1 region
    class R2 region
    class B boundary
```

> [!NOTE]
> The decision boundary is the locus of all points $\mathbf{x}$ for which $g_1(\mathbf{x}) = g_2(\mathbf{x})$. The shape of this boundary (linear, quadratic, piece-wise, …) is the geometric signature of the classifier family: a linear classifier produces a straight line, a quadratic classifier produces a conic, and a neural network can produce a closed region of arbitrary shape.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define Pattern Recognition. List any four real-world applications where it is used.**

**Model Answer:**

**Pattern Recognition (PR):** Pattern Recognition is the scientific discipline concerned with the automatic discovery of regularities in data and the subsequent classification of new data into one of a set of predefined categories, using computer algorithms trained on examples.

**Four real-world applications:**
1. **Handwritten digit recognition** in postal automation (MNIST benchmark).
2. **Face recognition** in smartphones, surveillance, and immigration control.
3. **Speech recognition** in virtual assistants (Siri, Google Assistant, Alexa).
4. **Medical diagnosis** from imaging (e.g., breast-cancer detection in mammograms).
5. *(Any one of: credit-card fraud detection, spam email filtering, biometric fingerprint matching, DNA sequence classification, industrial quality inspection, autonomous vehicle perception.)*

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**What are the main components of a typical pattern recognition system? Briefly explain each.**

**Model Answer:**

The main components of a typical PR system, in order of data flow, are:

1. **Sensor:** Captures the physical entity (image, sound, etc.) into a raw digital signal. *Example:* CCD camera, microphone, ECG electrode.
2. **Preprocessing:** Removes noise, normalizes intensity, aligns geometry. *Example:* histogram equalization, de-noising filters, scaling.
3. **Feature Extraction:** Transforms the preprocessed signal into a compact, discriminative numerical vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$. *Example:* PCA coefficients, MFCC, edge histograms.
4. **Classifier (Decision Module):** Maps the feature vector to one of $c$ class labels using a learned decision rule. *Example:* Bayes classifier, k-NN, SVM, neural network.
5. **Post-processing:** Refines the raw classifier output using context, costs, or domain knowledge. *Example:* spell-correction in OCR, language-model rescoring in speech.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's note:** Many students lose marks by forgetting the *training* component. A complete PR system has a **training pipeline** (separate from the test-time pipeline) in which the classifier's parameters are learned from a database of labelled examples. Always mention both pipelines.

---
