# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation: From Bayes' Theorem to Naive Bayes Posterior

We derive the Naive Bayes classification rule starting from first principles.

### Step 1 — Pattern Recognition Setup

Let $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_d)^T \in \mathbb{R}^d$ be a feature vector, and let $\Omega = \{\omega_1, \omega_2, \ldots, \omega_c\}$ be the set of $c$ possible class labels.

The **goal** of statistical pattern recognition is to find the **Bayes-optimal classifier**:

$$h^*(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\min_{h} \; \mathbb{E}[L(h(\mathbf{x}), \omega)]$$

where $L$ is a loss function. For the **0-1 loss** (penalizing every misclassification equally), this reduces to choosing the class with the highest posterior:

$$h^*(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_{\omega_j \in \Omega} \; P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x})$$

### Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Theorem

By the definition of conditional probability and the law of total probability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x}) &= \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)}{P(\mathbf{x})} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)}{\sum_{k=1}^{c} P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_k) P(\omega_k)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* The numerator uses the product rule of probability $P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) P(B)$. The denominator marginalizes the joint distribution $P(\mathbf{x}, \omega_j)$ over all classes via the law of total probability.

### Step 3 — Introduce the Naive Independence Assumption

We assume that features are conditionally independent given the class:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) &= P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_d \mid \omega_j) \\[6pt]
&= \prod_{i=1}^{d} P(x_i \mid \omega_j)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* The joint probability of $d$ conditionally independent events equals the product of their individual probabilities. This step reduces the estimation of $\mathcal{O}(k^d)$ parameters down to $\mathcal{O}(d \cdot c)$.

### Step 4 — Substitute Back into Bayes' Theorem

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x}) &= \frac{\left[\prod_{i=1}^{d} P(x_i \mid \omega_j)\right] P(\omega_j)}{P(\mathbf{x})} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{P(\omega_j) \prod_{i=1}^{d} P(x_i \mid \omega_j)}{P(\mathbf{x})}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Drop the Normalizing Constant

Since $P(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{k=1}^{c} P(\omega_k) \prod_{i=1}^{d} P(x_i \mid \omega_k)$ does not depend on $j$, the relative ordering of posteriors is preserved by ignoring it:

$$
P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(\omega_j) \prod_{i=1}^{d} P(x_i \mid \omega_j)
$$

### Step 6 — Final Naive Bayes Decision Rule

$$
h_{NB}(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_{\omega_j \in \Omega} \; P(\omega_j) \prod_{i=1}^{d} P(x_i \mid \omega_j)
$$

This is the **closed-form Naive Bayes classifier**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Given the following training data with features **Outlook** and **Wind**, classify the test instance $(\text{Sunny}, \text{Strong})$.

| Day | Outlook | Wind | Play |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sunny | Weak | No |
| 2 | Sunny | Strong | No |
| 3 | Overcast | Weak | Yes |
| 4 | Rain | Weak | Yes |
| 5 | Rain | Strong | No |
| 6 | Overcast | Strong | Yes |
| 7 | Sunny | Weak | Yes |
| 8 | Rain | Weak | Yes |
| 9 | Sunny | Strong | Yes |
| 10 | Overcast | Strong | Yes |

### Step 1 — Compute Class Priors

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Yes}) &= \frac{\vert \mathcal{D}_{\text{Yes}} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{D} \vert} = \frac{6}{10} = 0.6 \\[6pt]
P(\text{No}) &= \frac{\vert \mathcal{D}_{\text{No}} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{D} \vert} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Likelihoods (no smoothing first)

For class **Yes** (6 samples):
- $P(\text{Outlook=Sunny} \mid \text{Yes}) = \dfrac{1}{6}$
- $P(\text{Wind=Strong} \mid \text{Yes}) = \dfrac{2}{6} = \dfrac{1}{3}$

For class **No** (4 samples):
- $P(\text{Outlook=Sunny} \mid \text{No}) = \dfrac{2}{4} = \dfrac{1}{2}$
- $P(\text{Wind=Strong} \mid \text{No}) = \dfrac{2}{4} = \dfrac{1}{2}$

### Step 3 — Compute Posteriors (Unnormalized)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Yes} \mid \text{Sunny, Strong}) &\propto P(\text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{Yes}) \\[6pt]
&= 0.6 \times \frac{1}{6} \times \frac{1}{3} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{0.6}{18} = 0.0333
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{No} \mid \text{Sunny, Strong}) &\propto P(\text{No}) \cdot P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{No}) \cdot P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{No}) \\[6pt]
&= 0.4 \times \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{0.4}{4} = 0.1000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — MAP Decision

Since $P(\text{No} \mid \text{Sunny, Strong}) > P(\text{Yes} \mid \text{Sunny, Strong})$, classify as **No**.

### Step 5 — (Optional) Normalize Posteriors

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Yes} \mid \text{Sunny, Strong}) &= \frac{0.0333}{0.0333 + 0.1000} = 0.250 \\[6pt]
P(\text{No} \mid \text{Sunny, Strong}) &= \frac{0.1000}{0.0333 + 0.1000} = 0.750
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Example with Laplace Smoothing

Suppose $V_{\text{Outlook}} = 3$ (Sunny, Overcast, Rain) and $V_{\text{Wind}} = 2$ (Weak, Strong). Using $\alpha = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{P}(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Yes}) &= \frac{1 + 1}{6 + 3} = \frac{2}{9} \approx 0.222 \\[6pt]
\hat{P}(\text{Strong} \mid \text{Yes}) &= \frac{2 + 1}{6 + 2} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375 \\[6pt]
\hat{P}(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{No}) &= \frac{2 + 1}{4 + 3} = \frac{3}{7} \approx 0.429 \\[6pt]
\hat{P}(\text{Strong} \mid \text{No}) &= \frac{2 + 1}{4 + 2} = \frac{3}{6} = 0.500
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Boundary (Gaussian Naive Bayes)

For two classes $\omega_1, \omega_2$ with a single continuous feature $x$ modeled as Gaussian, the decision boundary $x^*$ is where the posteriors are equal:

$$
P(\omega_1) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_1^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x - \mu_1)^2}{2\sigma_1^2}\right) = P(\omega_2) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_2^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x - \mu_2)^2}{2\sigma_2^2}\right)
$$

Taking natural logarithms and rearranging:

$$
\log\frac{P(\omega_1)}{P(\omega_2)} - \frac{1}{2}\log\frac{\sigma_2^2}{\sigma_1^2} - \frac{(x - \mu_1)^2}{2\sigma_1^2} + \frac{(x - \mu_2)^2}{2\sigma_2^2} = 0
$$

When $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma$, the boundary simplifies to a **linear decision surface** (hyperplane), and Naive Bayes becomes equivalent to the **Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)** classifier.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** For two Gaussians with **equal variance**, the Naive Bayes decision boundary is **linear**. For **unequal variances**, the boundary is **quadratic**, equivalent to **Quadratic Discriminant Analysis (QDA)**. This is a frequently tested concept.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Naive Bayes Pipeline Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Feature Vector x = x1, x2, ..., xd] --> B[Training Phase: Estimate Parameters]
    B --> B1[Class Priors P of omega_j]
    B --> B2[Likelihoods P of xi given omega_j]
    B1 --> C[Trained Naive Bayes Model]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D[For Each Test Instance]
    D --> E[Compute Numerator for Class 1]
    D --> F[Compute Numerator for Class 2]
    D --> G[Compute Numerator for Class c]
    E --> H[argmax Selection MAP Decision]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Predicted Class Label omega hat]
```

## 4.2 Bayes' Theorem Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Prior P of omega_j] --> Combine[Multiply with Likelihood]
    P2[Likelihood P of x given omega_j] --> Combine
    Combine --> Num[Numerator: Joint Probability]
    Num --> Div[Divide by Evidence P of x]
    Div --> Post[Posterior P of omega_j given x]
    Post --> MAP[MAP Decision Rule]
    MAP --> Out[Classify Pattern x]
```

## 4.3 Naive Bayes Classification Flow with Smoothing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([New Test Sample x]) --> Split[Decompose into Features x1, x2, ..., xd]
    Split --> Loop[For Each Class omega_j in Omega]
    Loop --> Prior[Multiply Prior P of omega_j]
    Prior --> FeatureLoop[For Each Feature xi]
    FeatureLoop --> SmoothCheck{Zero Count?}
    SmoothCheck -- Yes --> LS[Apply Laplace Smoothing]
    SmoothCheck -- No --> Est[Use MLE Estimate]
    LS --> Product[Multiply to Running Product]
    Est --> Product
    FeatureLoop --> DoneAll{All Features?}
    DoneAll -- No --> FeatureLoop
    DoneAll -- Yes --> Store[Store Score for class omega_j]
    Store --> NextClass{All Classes?}
    NextClass -- No --> Loop
    NextClass -- Yes --> ArgMax[argmax over all class scores]
    ArgMax --> Predict([Final Predicted Class])
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Generative vs. Discriminative Perspective

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Generative_Model[Generative Approach Naive Bayes]
        G1[Models P of x and omega_j] --> G2[Estimates Full Joint Distribution]
        G2 --> G3[Factorizes via Independence Assumption]
        G3 --> G4[Derives Posterior via Bayes Theorem]
    end
    subgraph Discriminative_Model[Discriminative Approach Contrast]
        D1[Models P of omega_j given x Directly] --> D2[Logistic Regression]
        D2 --> D3[SVM Neural Networks]
    end
    G4 --> Compare{Both Produce Decision Boundary}
    D3 --> Compare
    Compare --> Result[Different Geometric Properties]
```

## 4.5 Decision Boundary Topology (Continuous Features)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[Feature Space R to the power d] --> Check{Class Variance Assumption}
    Check -- Equal Variance --> Linear[Linear Decision Boundary]
    Check -- Unequal Variance --> Quad[Quadratic Decision Boundary]
    Linear --> Eq1[Equivalent to LDA]
    Quad --> Eq2[Equivalent to QDA]
    Eq1 --> NB1[Naive Bayes Special Case 1]
    Eq2 --> NB2[Naive Bayes Special Case 2]
```

## 4.6 Conditional Independence Graphical Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C[Class Node omega_j] --> X1[Feature x1]
    C --> X2[Feature x2]
    C --> X3[Feature x3]
    C --> Xd[Feature xd]
    note1[No Edges Between Features Implies Conditional Independence Given Class]
    C -.-> note1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Graph:** In the graphical model, the class node $\omega_j$ is the **parent** of every feature node. The **absence of edges** between feature nodes $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_d$ is precisely what encodes the naive conditional independence assumption $x_i \perp x_k \mid \omega_j$ for all $i \neq k$.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q:** Define the **Naive Bayes classifier**. Why is it called "naive"?

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Naive Bayes is a probabilistic supervised learning algorithm based on Bayes' Theorem that classifies a pattern by computing the posterior probability of each class given the feature vector and assigning the class with the highest posterior probability (MAP decision rule).
>
> **Why Naive (1 mark):** It is called "naive" because it makes the simplifying assumption that all features are **conditionally independent** of each other given the class label, i.e., $P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_d \mid \omega_j) = \prod_{i=1}^{d} P(x_i \mid \omega_j)$. This assumption is rarely true in real-world data, hence the name.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q:** What is the **zero-frequency problem** in Naive Bayes? How is it solved?

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Zero-Frequency Problem (1.5 marks):** If a categorical feature value $v$ never appears in the training samples for a particular class $\omega_j$, then the Maximum Likelihood Estimate $P(x_i = v \mid \omega_j) = 0$. Since the Naive Bayes classifier multiplies all likelihoods together, this single zero value forces the entire posterior to zero, causing the model to **never predict** that class for that feature combination — even if other features strongly suggest it.
>
> **Solution: Laplace (Additive) Smoothing (1.5 marks):** Add a smoothing parameter $\alpha > 0$ (typically $\alpha = 1$) to every count: $\hat{P}(x_i = v \mid \omega_j) = \dfrac{\text{count}(x_i = v, \omega_j) + \alpha}{\text{count}(\omega_j) + \alpha \cdot V}$ where $V$ is the vocabulary size. This ensures no probability is ever exactly zero.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** State and prove **Bayes' Theorem** for pattern classification. Explain the significance of each term in the equation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Naive Bayes decision rule** from first principles. Explain the conditional independence assumption with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Bayes' Theorem Statement (1 mark):**

For events $A$ and $B$ where $P(B) > 0$:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \, P(A)}{P(B)}$$

**Proof (4 marks):**

By the definition of conditional probability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(A \mid B) &= \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} \quad \text{(definition of conditional probability)} \\[6pt]
P(B \mid A) &= \frac{P(B \cap A)}{P(A)} \quad \text{(definition of conditional probability)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $P(A \cap B) = P(B \cap A)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(A \cap B) &= P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) \\[6pt]
\text{Substituting into the first equation:} \quad P(A \mid B) &= \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Significance of Terms (2 marks):**

In the pattern recognition context $P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$:

- **Prior $P(\omega_j)$:** Probability of class $\omega_j$ before observing data; reflects the base rate.
- **Likelihood $P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j)$:** Probability of observing the feature vector $\mathbf{x}$ assuming the class is $\omega_j$.
- **Evidence $P(\mathbf{x})$:** Normalizing constant ensuring probabilities sum to 1.
- **Posterior $P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x})$:** Updated belief about class $\omega_j$ after observing evidence $\mathbf{x}$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Starting from Bayes' theorem:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x}) &= \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)}{P(\mathbf{x})} \quad \text{(Bayes' Theorem)} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\left[\prod_{i=1}^{d} P(x_i \mid \omega_j)\right] P(\omega_j)}{P(\mathbf{x})} \quad \text{(Naive Independence Assumption)} \\[6pt]
&\propto P(\omega_j) \prod_{i=1}^{d} P(x_i \mid \omega_j) \quad \text{(Dropping Normalizer)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The Naive Bayes MAP classifier is:

$$h_{NB}(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_{\omega_j} \; P(\omega_j) \prod_{i=1}^{d} P(x_i \mid \omega_j)$$

**Conditional Independence Assumption Explanation (3 marks):**

The assumption states that for a given class $\omega_j$, knowing the value of feature $x_1$ provides no information about $x_2$:

$$P(x_1, x_2 \mid \omega_j) = P(x_1 \mid \omega_j) \cdot P(x_2 \mid \omega_j)$$

**Example:** In spam email classification, suppose features are "contains the word *free*" ($x_1$) and "contains the word *money*" ($x_2$). In reality, these features are highly correlated. However, Naive Bayes assumes that for a spam email, the presence of "free" does not affect the probability of seeing "money". This simplification dramatically reduces the parameters to be learned from exponential to linear in the number of features.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Bayesian framework for pattern classification**. Discuss the role of **prior**, **likelihood**, and **posterior** probabilities. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given the training data below, build a **Naive Bayes classifier** to predict the class of the test instance $(\text{Sunny}, \text{Strong})$. Use **Laplace smoothing** with $\alpha = 1$. Show all calculations. **(7 Marks)**

| Day | Outlook | Wind | Play |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sunny | Weak | No |
| 2 | Sunny | Strong | No |
| 3 | Overcast | Weak | Yes |
| 4 | Rain | Weak | Yes |
| 5 | Rain | Strong | No |
| 6 | Overcast | Strong | Yes |
| 7 | Sunny | Weak | Yes |
| 8 | Rain | Weak | Yes |
| 9 | Sunny | Strong | Yes |
| 10 | Overcast | Strong | Yes |

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Bayesian Framework Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Prior P(ω_j) ──┐
                ├──► Multiply ──► P(ω_j)·P(x│ω_j) ──► Divide by P(x) ──► Posterior P(ω_j│x)
Likelihood P(x│ω_j) ──┘                                                       │
                                                                              ▼
                                                                       MAP Decision
                                                                       argmax ω_j P(ω_j│x)
```

**Role of Components (4 marks):**

- **Prior $P(\omega_j)$:** Encodes prior knowledge or base rates of classes without observing the pattern. Estimated as $P(\omega_j) = \dfrac{\vert \mathcal{D}_{\omega_j} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{D} \vert}$.
- **Likelihood $P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j)$:** Models how likely we are to see the feature vector if the pattern belongs to class $\omega_j$. Captures the class-specific feature distribution.
- **Posterior $P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x})$:** Updated probability of class membership after observing evidence $\mathbf{x}$. Used directly for the MAP classification decision.
- **Decision Rule:** Pattern $\mathbf{x}$ is assigned to class $\omega^*$ if $P(\omega^* \mid \mathbf{x}) = \max_j P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x})$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Class Priors (1 mark):**

$$
P(\text{Yes}) = \frac{6}{10} = 0.6, \quad P(\text{No}) = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4
$$

**Step 2 — Vocabulary Sizes:**

$V_{\text{Outlook}} = 3$ (Sunny, Overcast, Rain); $V_{\text{Wind}} = 2$ (Weak, Strong).

**Step 3 — Smoothed Likelihoods (3 marks):**

For class **Yes** (6 samples):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{P}(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Yes}) &= \frac{1 + 1}{6 + 3} = \frac{2}{9} \\[6pt]
\hat{P}(\text{Strong} \mid \text{Yes}) &= \frac{2 + 1}{6 + 2} = \frac{3}{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

For class **No** (4 samples):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{P}(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{No}) &= \frac{2 + 1}{4 + 3} = \frac{3}{7} \\[6pt]
\hat{P}(\text{Strong} \mid \text{No}) &= \frac{2 + 1}{4 + 2} = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Numerators (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Yes}) \cdot \hat{P}(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Yes}) \cdot \hat{P}(\text{Strong} \mid \text{Yes}) &= 0.6 \times \frac{2}{9} \times \frac{3}{8} \\[6pt]
&= 0.6 \times 0.0833 = 0.0500
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{No}) \cdot \hat{P}(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{No}) \cdot \hat{P}(\text{Strong} \mid \text{No}) &= 0.4 \times \frac{3}{7} \times \frac{1}{2} \\[6pt]
&= 0.4 \times 0.2143 = 0.0857
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Normalize (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Yes} \mid \text{Sunny, Strong}) &= \frac{0.0500}{0.0500 + 0.0857} \approx 0.368 \\[6pt]
P(\text{No} \mid \text{Sunny, Strong}) &= \frac{0.0857}{0.0500 + 0.0857} \approx 0.632
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Since $P(\text{No} \mid \text{Sunny, Strong}) > P(\text{Yes} \mid \text{Sunny, Strong})$, classify as **No**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Laplace Smoothing (–2 marks):** If the training data contains a feature-class combination unseen in training, the entire posterior becomes zero. Always check for this and apply smoothing.
> 2. **Not dropping $P(\mathbf{x})$ correctly (–1 mark):** When the question asks for classification, the denominator $P(\mathbf{x})$ can be ignored, but if the question asks for actual posterior values, normalization is mandatory.
> 3. **Confusing priors with posteriors (–1 mark):** $P(\omega_j)$ is the *prior*; $P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x})$ is the *posterior*. Mixing these up is a frequent error.
> 4. **Sign error in exponent of Gaussian (–1 mark):** Ensure the Gaussian likelihood uses $\exp\left(-\dfrac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ with the correct negative sign.
> 5. **Arithmetic mistakes in smoothing (–1 mark):** Always remember to add $\alpha \cdot V$ to the **denominator**, not just $\alpha$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Naive Bayes**

### Core Concepts
- ⭐ Naive Bayes is a **generative probabilistic classifier** based on Bayes' Theorem.
- ⭐ The **naive assumption** is **conditional independence** of features given the class: $P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) = \prod_{i=1}^{d} P(x_i \mid \omega_j)$.
- ⭐ The decision rule is **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)**: assign $\mathbf{x}$ to class with highest $P(\omega_j) \prod_{i} P(x_i \mid \omega_j)$.
- ⭐ The normalizing constant $P(\mathbf{x})$ is **discarded** during classification but needed for computing actual posterior values.

### Key Formulae to Memorize
- 📌 Bayes' Theorem: $P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$
- 📌 Naive Bayes Posterior: $P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(\omega_j) \prod_{i=1}^{d} P(x_i \mid \omega_j)$
- 📌 Gaussian Likelihood: $P(x_i \mid \omega_j) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_{ij}^2}} \exp\left(-\dfrac{(x_i - \mu_{ij})^2}{2\sigma_{ij}^2}\right)$
- 📌 Laplace Smoothing: $\hat{P}(x_i = v \mid \omega_j) = \dfrac{\text{count}(x_i = v, \omega_j) + \alpha}{\text{count}(\omega_j) + \alpha \cdot V}$
- 📌 MLE Prior: $P(\omega_j) = \dfrac{\vert \mathcal{D}_{\omega_j} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{D} \vert}$

### Variants
- 📍 **Gaussian NB:** Continuous features with Normal distribution assumption.
- 📍 **Multinomial NB:** Count-based features (e.g., word frequencies in text).
- 📍 **Bernoulli NB:** Binary features (e.g., word presence/absence).

### Important Properties
- 🔹 Despite the simplistic assumption, Naive Bayes works surprisingly well in practice.
- 🔹 It requires **linear** $\mathcal{O}(d \cdot c)$ parameters instead of exponential.
- 🔹 Performs well with **high-dimensional sparse data** like text.
- 🔹 Equivalent to **LDA** (linear boundary) when Gaussian variances are equal.
- 🔹 Equivalent to **QDA** (quadratic boundary) when Gaussian variances differ.
- 🔹 Output probabilities are not well-calibrated but **rank order is preserved**.
- 🔹 Use **log-space** computation $\log P(\omega_j) + \sum_i \log P(x_i \mid \omega_j)$ to avoid numerical underflow.

### Common Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Confusing likelihood $P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j)$ with posterior $P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x})$.
- ❌ Forgetting to apply Laplace smoothing for unseen feature values.
- ❌ Including the normalizing constant $P(\mathbf{x})$ when only the **decision** is required.
- ❌ Treating Naive Bayes as if features are **marginally independent** (they are conditionally independent given the class, not marginally).
- ❌ Computing the Gaussian likelihood with the **wrong variance** or missing the $\sqrt{2\pi}$ factor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensionality Reduction (PCA and LDA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT415 - FOUNDATIONS OF PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dimensionality Reduction (PCA and LDA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — DIMENSIONALITY REDUCTION (PCA AND LDA)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Dimensionality Reduction — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Dimensionality Reduction** is a process in pattern recognition and machine learning that transforms high-dimensional data (with many features) into a lower-dimensional representation while preserving as much of the salient, discriminative, and informative structure of the original data as possible.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, dimensionality reduction is studied as a **preprocessing and feature extraction** stage in any pattern recognition pipeline (PRD — Pattern Recognition Design). It serves two principal purposes:

1. **Computational efficiency** — fewer dimensions mean lower memory consumption, faster training, and reduced algorithmic complexity.
2. **Statistical robustness** — high-dimensional spaces suffer from the **Curse of Dimensionality** (Bellman, 1961), where data becomes sparse, distances lose meaning, and classifiers overfit.

The two principal **linear, unsupervised/supervised** techniques mandated by the KTU PEADT415 syllabus are:
- **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** — *unsupervised, variance-preserving* projection.
- **Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)** — *supervised, class-separation-preserving* projection.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Shadow on the Wall

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine holding a transparent 3D wireframe cube in front of a flashlight, with a flat wall behind it. The 2D shadow cast on the wall is a *projection* of the 3D object onto a lower-dimensional surface. The art of dimensionality reduction is choosing the **angle** at which you hold the object so that the resulting shadow keeps the *most distinguishing features* of the original. A shadow of a cube held face-on is just a square (loses depth information). A shadow held diagonally reveals the 3D nature more richly. **PCA** chooses the angle that *maximizes the spread/variance* of the projected data, while **LDA** chooses the angle that *maximizes the separation between different labelled groups*.

A more granular analogy:
- **PCA** is like a *photographer* choosing the best camera angle to make a portrait look as detailed and varied as possible.
- **LDA** is like a *judge at a flower show* choosing a viewing angle that best *separates* the red roses from the white lilies on display.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning in KTU Context |
|---|---|
| **Feature Vector** $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{D}$ | A single data sample with **D** original measurements/features. |
| **Reduced Vector** $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ | The same sample after projection into a $d$-dimensional subspace, where $d \ll D$. |
| **Projection Matrix** $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times d}$ | A matrix whose columns are the basis vectors of the new subspace. |
| **Eigenvalues** $\lambda_i$ | Scalars representing the variance (PCA) or class-separation strength (LDA) along each direction. |
| **Eigenvectors** $\mathbf{w}_i$ | The unit-length basis directions of the optimal subspace. |
| **Scatter Matrix** | A covariance-like matrix quantifying the spread of data (used in both PCA and LDA). |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard metric used to quantify the loss in dimensionality reduction is the **Reconstruction Error** (for PCA) given by $J = \sum_i \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i \Vert^{2}$ where $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_i$ is the reconstructed sample.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principal Component Analysis (PCA) — Theory

### 2.1.1 What is PCA?

PCA is an **orthogonal linear transformation** that maps the data into a new coordinate system such that:
- The **first principal component** has the **largest possible variance**.
- Each subsequent component has the **next highest variance** and is **orthogonal** to all previous ones.

It solves a fundamental problem: *given correlated high-dimensional data, can we find uncorrelated, variance-ordered axes that summarize the data compactly?*

### 2.1.2 Operational Steps of PCA

1. **Center the Data** — Subtract the mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ from every sample so that the data has zero empirical mean. This is critical because PCA is sensitive to translation.
2. **Compute the Covariance Matrix** — Build the $D \times D$ matrix $\mathbf{S} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})^{T}$.
3. **Eigendecomposition** — Solve $\mathbf{S}\mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$ to obtain the eigenvectors and eigenvalues of $\mathbf{S}$.
4. **Sort and Select** — Rank the eigenvectors by descending eigenvalue. Select the top-$d$ eigenvectors to form the projection matrix $\mathbf{W}$.
5. **Project** — Compute the low-dimensional embedding $\mathbf{y}_i = \mathbf{W}^{T} \mathbf{x}_i$ for each sample.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why eigenvalues matter:** An eigenvalue $\lambda_k$ tells you *how much variance* of the original data is captured along its eigenvector $\mathbf{w}_k$. Picking eigenvectors with the largest eigenvalues is mathematically equivalent to *minimizing the mean squared reconstruction error* — this is the **optimality property of PCA** (proven via Lagrange multipliers).

### 2.1.3 Dual Derivation Viewpoints

PCA can be derived from **two equivalent perspectives** that students must know for KTU 14-mark questions:

- **Maximization View (Variance Maximization):** Find $\mathbf{w}$ that maximizes $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{S}\mathbf{w}$ subject to $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{w} = 1$.
- **Minimization View (Reconstruction Error Minimization):** Find $\mathbf{w}$ that minimizes $\sum_i \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - (\mathbf{w}\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}_i) \Vert^{2}$.

Both lead to the same eigenproblem $\mathbf{S}\mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$.

---

## 2.2 Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) — Theory

### 2.2.1 What is LDA?

LDA is a **supervised** linear dimensionality reduction method that finds the projection direction(s) which **best separate two or more classes**. Unlike PCA, which only uses the input features, LDA uses the **class labels** to find a subspace that maximizes *between-class separability* while minimizing *within-class scatter*.

LDA is also known as **Fisher's Linear Discriminant** (FLD) in the two-class case, named after **R. A. Fisher** who formalized it in 1936.

### 2.2.2 Operational Steps of LDA

1. **Compute Class Means** — For each class $c \in \{1, \dots, C\}$, calculate the mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu}_c$ of samples belonging to that class.
2. **Compute Within-Class Scatter Matrix** $\mathbf{S}_W$ — Sum of covariance matrices of each class.
3. **Compute Between-Class Scatter Matrix** $\mathbf{S}_B$ — Scatter of class means around the global mean, weighted by class counts.
4. **Solve the Generalized Eigenvalue Problem** $\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}$.
5. **Select Top Eigenvectors** — At most $C - 1$ non-trivial eigenvectors exist (this is the famous LDA limit).
6. **Project** — $\mathbf{y}_i = \mathbf{W}^{T} \mathbf{x}_i$.

### 2.2.3 The Fisher Criterion

The objective function maximized by LDA is the **Fisher criterion** (Rayleigh quotient):

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}}
$$

Taking the derivative and setting it to zero gives the generalized eigenproblem above.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the $C-1$ limit?** $\mathbf{S}_B$ has rank at most $C - 1$ because it is constructed from $C$ class means that all lie in a $(C-1)$-dimensional affine subspace (the rank deficiency comes from the constraint that the mean of means equals the global mean). Hence LDA can produce at most $C - 1$ useful projection directions.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean Vector | $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{x}_i$ | Population/sample mean of all data |
| 2 | Centered Data | $\mathbf{x}_i^{c} = \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}$ | Zero-mean transform |
| 3 | Sample Covariance (PCA) | $\mathbf{S} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})^{T}$ | Used in PCA |
| 4 | Eigendecomposition | $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{W}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{W}^{T}$ | $\mathbf{W}$ orthogonal, $\boldsymbol{\Lambda}$ diagonal |
| 5 | Reconstruction Error | $J = \sum_i \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{W}\mathbf{W}^{T}\mathbf{x}_i \Vert^{2}$ | Minimized by top-$d$ eigenvectors |
| 6 | Variance Retained | $\eta = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{D} \lambda_i}$ | Ratio of preserved variance |
| 7 | Within-Class Scatter | $\mathbf{S}_W = \sum_{c=1}^{C} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathcal{X}_c} (\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c)(\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c)^{T}$ | Sum of intra-class covariances |
| 8 | Between-Class Scatter | $\mathbf{S}_B = \sum_{c=1}^{C} N_c (\boldsymbol{\mu}_c - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_c - \boldsymbol{\mu})^{T}$ | Weighted spread of class means |
| 9 | Total Scatter | $\mathbf{S}_T = \mathbf{S}_W + \mathbf{S}_B$ | Invariant decomposition |
| 10 | Fisher Criterion | $J(\mathbf{w}) = \dfrac{\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}}$ | Maximized by LDA |
| 11 | Generalized Eigenproblem | $\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}$ | Equivalent to $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1}\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$ if $\mathbf{S}_W$ invertible |
| 12 | Max LDA Components | $d_{\max} = C - 1$ | For $C$ classes |
| 13 | Projection | $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{W}^{T} \mathbf{x}$ | Final low-dimensional code |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** PCA is widely used in *image compression* (e.g., JPEG uses a related DCT transform), *face recognition* (Eigenfaces, Turk & Pentland), *gene expression analysis* in bioinformatics, and *anomaly detection*. LDA is the workhorse of *speech recognition* (classic Gaussian Mixture + LDA pipelines), *medical diagnosis* (separating diseased vs. healthy tissue), and *handwriting recognition*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of PCA via Variance Maximization

We start with centered data $\{\mathbf{x}_i^{c}\}_{i=1}^{N}$ and want to find a unit vector $\mathbf{w}$ such that the variance of the projected data $\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{x}_i^{c}$ is maximized.

**Step 1 — Define the variance of the projected data:**

The projection of sample $i$ onto direction $\mathbf{w}$ is the scalar $y_i = \mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{x}_i^{c}$. The empirical variance of the projected data is:

$$
\sigma^{2} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \bar{y})^{2}
$$

Because the data is centered ($\bar{\mathbf{x}}^{c} = \mathbf{0}$), the projected mean is also zero:

$$
\bar{y} = \mathbf{w}^{T} \bar{\mathbf{x}}^{c} = \mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{0} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Express the variance in matrix form:**

Since $\bar{y} = 0$, the variance simplifies to:

$$
\sigma^{2} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i^{2} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{x}_i^{c})(\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{x}_i^{c})^{T}
$$

Because $y_i$ is a scalar, $(\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{x}_i^{c})^{T} = \mathbf{x}_i^{c\,T} \mathbf{w}$, so:

$$
\sigma^{2} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{x}_i^{c} \mathbf{x}_i^{c\,T} \mathbf{w}
$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $\mathbf{w}$ to get the Rayleigh quotient:**

$$
\sigma^{2} = \mathbf{w}^{T} \left[ \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{x}_i^{c} \mathbf{x}_i^{c\,T} \right] \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{S} \mathbf{w}
$$

**Step 4 — Formulate the constrained optimization:**

We want to maximize $\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{S} \mathbf{w}$ subject to $\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{w} = 1$ (so that $\mathbf{w}$ is a unit direction). The Lagrangian is:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{S} \mathbf{w} - \lambda (\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{w} - 1)
$$

**Step 5 — Take the gradient with respect to $\mathbf{w}$ and set it to zero:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 2\mathbf{S}\mathbf{w} - 2\lambda \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}
$$

This rearranges to the classical **eigenvalue problem**:

$$
\mathbf{S}\mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result:**

- The stationary points of the variance are the eigenvectors of $\mathbf{S}$.
- The variance along eigenvector $\mathbf{w}_k$ equals its eigenvalue $\lambda_k$.
- To maximize variance, pick the eigenvector with the *largest* eigenvalue.
- The next principal component is the eigenvector with the second largest eigenvalue, and so on, while enforcing orthogonality (which the eigendecomposition guarantees since $\mathbf{S}$ is real symmetric).

**Step 7 — Optimality proof (top-$d$ selection):**

Suppose we have already chosen $d$ eigenvectors $\{\mathbf{w}_1, \dots, \mathbf{w}_d\}$ forming the columns of $\mathbf{W}$. The total variance captured is:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{d} \lambda_k = \mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{W}^{T} \mathbf{S} \mathbf{W})
$$

By the **Poincaré separation theorem**, the maximum of this trace over all rank-$d$ orthonormal $\mathbf{W}$ is achieved precisely when $\mathbf{W}$ contains the $d$ eigenvectors corresponding to the top-$d$ eigenvalues. This proves that selecting the top-$d$ eigenvectors is globally optimal for variance retention.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of LDA (Two-Class Fisher Criterion)

We consider the simplest case with $C = 2$ classes $\mathcal{X}_1$ and $\mathcal{X}_2$ having $N_1$ and $N_2$ samples respectively.

**Step 1 — Define class means and global mean:**

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = \frac{1}{N_1}\sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathcal{X}_1} \mathbf{x}_i, \quad \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = \frac{1}{N_2}\sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathcal{X}_2} \mathbf{x}_i, \quad \boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{x}_i
$$

**Step 2 — Construct within-class scatter matrix:**

$$
\mathbf{S}_W = \sum_{c=1}^{2} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathcal{X}_c} (\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c)(\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c)^{T}
$$

**Step 3 — Construct between-class scatter matrix:**

$$
\mathbf{S}_B = \sum_{c=1}^{2} N_c (\boldsymbol{\mu}_c - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_c - \boldsymbol{\mu})^{T} = N_1 (\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu})^{T} + N_2 (\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu})^{T}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the scatter of the projected data:**

When the data is projected onto $\mathbf{w}$, the projected mean of class $c$ is $\tilde{\mu}_c = \mathbf{w}^{T} \boldsymbol{\mu}_c$. The between-class scatter of the projected data is:

$$
\tilde{S}_B = (\tilde{\mu}_1 - \tilde{\mu}_2)^{2} = \mathbf{w}^{T} (\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)(\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)^{T} \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}
$$

(using the fact that for $C=2$, $\mathbf{S}_B$ reduces to this scaled form). The within-class scatter after projection is:

$$
\tilde{S}_W = \sum_{c=1}^{2} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathcal{X}_c} (\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{w}^{T} \boldsymbol{\mu}_c)^{2} = \mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}
$$

**Step 5 — Formulate the Fisher criterion:**

We want to maximize the *ratio* of between-class scatter to within-class scatter:

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}}
$$

**Step 6 — Take the derivative and set to zero:**

Note that $J$ is invariant to scaling of $\mathbf{w}$, so we can fix the denominator $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w} = 1$ and maximize the numerator. The Lagrangian is:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} - \lambda (\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w} - 1)
$$

Taking the gradient with respect to $\mathbf{w}$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 2\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} - 2\lambda \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}
$$

Rearranging yields the **generalized eigenproblem**:

$$
\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve the eigenproblem:**

If $\mathbf{S}_W$ is non-singular, we can multiply both sides by $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1}$:

$$
\mathbf{S}_W^{-1} \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}
$$

This is a standard eigenvalue problem in the matrix $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1}\mathbf{S}_B$. The eigenvectors are the LDA projection directions, and the eigenvalues measure the *Fisher ratio* along each direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-class special case:** For $C=2$, the rank of $\mathbf{S}_B$ is 1, so there is exactly one non-trivial eigenvector. The closed-form solution is:
> $$\mathbf{w} \propto \mathbf{S}_W^{-1} (\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)$$
> This is the famous **Fisher linear discriminant** direction and frequently appears in KTU 14-mark problems.

---

## 3.3 PCA vs. LDA — Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | PCA | LDA |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Unsupervised | Supervised |
| **Objective** | Maximize variance | Maximize class separation |
| **Uses labels?** | No | Yes |
| **Scatter matrix** | Total covariance $\mathbf{S}$ | $\mathbf{S}_W, \mathbf{S}_B$ |
| **Max components** | $\min(N, D)$ | $C - 1$ |
| **Eigenproblem** | $\mathbf{S}\mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$ | $\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}$ |
| **Geometric view** | Best fit line through cloud | Best separating hyperplane |
| **Failure mode** | Loses class info if low-variance class directions exist | Fails when $\mathbf{S}_W$ is singular (small sample size) |
| **Variance captured** | Yes (analytically via eigenvalues) | No direct measure |
| **Class separation** | Not guaranteed | Optimized directly |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PCA Pipeline — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw High Dimensional Data X in R D]:::input
    B[Step 1 Mean Centering subtract mu]:::proc
    C[Step 2 Compute Covariance Matrix S]:::proc
    D[Step 3 Eigendecomposition S W equals lambda W]:::proc
    E[Step 4 Sort Eigenvalues Descending]:::proc
    F[Step 5 Select Top d Eigenvectors]:::proc
    G[Step 6 Build Projection Matrix W]:::proc
    H[Step 7 Project Y equals W transpose X]:::output
    I[Low Dimensional Embedding in R d]:::output

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I

    classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef proc fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 LDA Pipeline — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Labelled Training Data with Class Info]:::input
    B[Step 1 Compute Class Means mu sub c]:::proc
    C[Step 2 Within Class Scatter S W]:::proc
    D[Step 3 Between Class Scatter S B]:::proc
    E[Step 4 Solve Generalised Eigenvalue Problem]:::proc
    F[Step 5 Select Top C minus 1 Eigenvectors]:::proc
    G[Step 6 Build Projection Matrix W]:::proc
    H[Step 7 Project Y equals W transpose X]:::output
    I[Discriminative Low Dimensional Embedding]:::output

    A --> B
    B --> C
    B --> D
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I

    classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef proc fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 PCA vs LDA Conceptual Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original 2D Data]:::startnode
    P[PCA Direction Maximum Variance]:::pcaclass
    L[LDA Direction Maximum Class Separation]:::ldaclass
    PV[Projection Axis P]:::pcaclass
    LV[Projection Axis L]:::ldaclass

    A --> P
    A --> L
    P --> PV
    L --> LV

    classDef startnode fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef pcaclass fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef ldaclass fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

## 4.4 Mathematical Architecture — Eigendecomposition Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Symmetric Matrix S D by D]:::input
    B[Compute Characteristic Polynomial det S minus lambda I equals 0]:::proc
    C[Obtain Eigenvalues lambda 1 to lambda D]:::proc
    D[Solve S minus lambda I times w equals 0 for each lambda]:::proc
    E[Get Eigenvectors w 1 to w D]:::proc
    F[Form Eigenvector Matrix W and Diagonal Lambda]:::proc
    G[Verify S equals W Lambda W transpose]:::output

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G

    classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef proc fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the concept of the Curse of Dimensionality in pattern recognition. How does dimensionality reduction address it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Curse of Dimensionality**, a term coined by Richard Bellman, refers to the various phenomena that arise when analyzing data in high-dimensional spaces. As the number of features $D$ grows, the volume of the feature space grows exponentially, causing data to become extremely sparse. Distances between points lose their discriminative power (all pairwise distances converge), statistical estimates become unreliable due to insufficient samples per region, and classifiers tend to **overfit** the training data. Dimensionality reduction addresses this by projecting the data into a lower-dimensional subspace $d \ll D$ using methods like PCA (which preserves variance) or LDA (which preserves class separability), thereby yielding denser, more meaningful feature representations. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between feature selection and feature extraction. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Feature Selection** chooses a *subset* of the original features based on criteria such as correlation, mutual information, or statistical tests, while **Feature Extraction** *transforms* the original features into a new set (typically lower-dimensional) via linear or non-linear mappings. Example of feature selection: choosing the top 10 genes with the highest t-test scores in microarray analysis. Example of feature extraction: projecting 1000-dimensional pixel data onto 50 principal components via PCA. Feature extraction is preferred when all original features carry some information but redundancy exists. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — PCA-Focused

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice A]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** algorithm. Clearly state the objective function and the role of eigenvalues in determining the principal components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider five 2-dimensional data points (already centered): $\mathbf{x}_1 = (1, 2), \mathbf{x}_2 = (2, 1), \mathbf{x}_3 = (-1, -2), \mathbf{x}_4 = (-2, -1), \mathbf{x}_5 = (0, 0)$. Compute the covariance matrix and find the principal components. Project the data onto the first principal component. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Introduction:** PCA is an orthogonal linear transformation that finds a new coordinate system in which the variance of the data is maximized along the first axis, the second axis, and so on, with each axis being orthogonal to the previous ones. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Objective Function:**

Given centered data $\mathbf{x}_i^{c}$, find $\mathbf{w}$ that maximizes:

$$
\max_{\mathbf{w}} \; \mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{S} \mathbf{w} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{w} = 1
$$

where $\mathbf{S} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i \mathbf{x}_i^{c} \mathbf{x}_i^{c\,T}$ is the covariance matrix. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Algorithm Steps (with diagram):**

1. Compute the mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and center the data.
2. Compute the covariance matrix $\mathbf{S}$.
3. Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of $\mathbf{S}$.
4. Sort eigenvectors in descending order of eigenvalues.
5. Form the projection matrix $\mathbf{W}$ from the top-$d$ eigenvectors.
6. Compute $\mathbf{y}_i = \mathbf{W}^{T} \mathbf{x}_i^{c}$.

(2 marks for steps and diagram)

**Step 4 — Role of Eigenvalues:** Each eigenvalue $\lambda_k$ equals the variance of the data along the corresponding eigenvector $\mathbf{w}_k$. Therefore, eigenvectors with the largest eigenvalues capture the most variance, and the sum of the top-$d$ eigenvalues divided by the sum of all eigenvalues gives the *proportion of variance retained*. (2 marks)

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** PCA is optimal in the minimum mean squared reconstruction error sense and forms the foundation of many unsupervised dimensionality reduction pipelines. (1 mark)

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Verify that the data is already centered:** The sum of all coordinates: $(1+2-1-2+0, 2+1-2-1+0) = (0, 0)$. So $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (0, 0)$. (0.5 marks)

**Step 2 — Compute the covariance matrix:**

$$
\mathbf{S} = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5} \begin{bmatrix} x_{i1}^{2} & x_{i1} x_{i2} \\ x_{i1} x_{i2} & x_{i2}^{2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute individual terms:
- $\mathbf{x}_1 \mathbf{x}_1^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$
- $\mathbf{x}_2 \mathbf{x}_2^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
- $\mathbf{x}_3 \mathbf{x}_3^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$
- $\mathbf{x}_4 \mathbf{x}_4^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
- $\mathbf{x}_5 \mathbf{x}_5^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$

Sum:

$$
\sum_i \mathbf{x}_i \mathbf{x}_i^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 8 \\ 8 & 10 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Covariance matrix:

$$
\mathbf{S} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{bmatrix} 10 & 8 \\ 8 & 10 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1.6 \\ 1.6 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 3 — Eigendecomposition:**

The characteristic equation: $\det(\mathbf{S} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$:

$$
(2 - \lambda)^{2} - (1.6)^{2} = 0 \Rightarrow (2 - \lambda)^{2} = 2.56 \Rightarrow 2 - \lambda = \pm 1.6
$$

So $\lambda_1 = 3.6$ and $\lambda_2 = 0.4$. (1.5 marks)

**Step 4 — Find eigenvectors:**

For $\lambda_1 = 3.6$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} -1.6 & 1.6 \\ 1.6 & -1.6 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{w} = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{w}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

For $\lambda_2 = 0.4$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1.6 & 1.6 \\ 1.6 & 1.6 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{w} = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{w}_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

(1.5 marks)

**Step 5 — Project onto first principal component:**

Projection of $\mathbf{x}_i$ onto $\mathbf{w}_1$ is $y_i = \mathbf{w}_1^{T} \mathbf{x}_i = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(x_{i1} + x_{i2})$:

- $y_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1 + 2) = \frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}$
- $y_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(2 + 1) = \frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}$
- $y_3 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(-1 - 2) = -\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}$
- $y_4 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(-2 - 1) = -\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}$
- $y_5 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(0 + 0) = 0$

(1.5 marks)

---

### Question B — LDA-Focused

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2 Choice B]**

**(a)** With suitable diagrams, explain **Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)** for multi-class classification. Derive the Fisher criterion and explain the constraint on the maximum number of projection directions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two classes have the following 2-D data (assume $N_1 = N_2 = 3$):
- Class 1: $\{(1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 2)\}$
- Class 2: $\{(6, 5), (7, 6), (5, 7)\}$

Compute the within-class and between-class scatter matrices, then determine the Fisher linear discriminant direction $\mathbf{w}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Introduction:** LDA is a supervised linear dimensionality reduction technique that finds projection directions maximizing *between-class* scatter relative to *within-class* scatter. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Define Scatter Matrices:**

For $C$ classes, with means $\boldsymbol{\mu}_c$ and global mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}$:

$$
\mathbf{S}_W = \sum_{c=1}^{C} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathcal{X}_c} (\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c)(\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c)^{T}
$$

$$
\mathbf{S}_B = \sum_{c=1}^{C} N_c (\boldsymbol{\mu}_c - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_c - \boldsymbol{\mu})^{T}
$$

(1.5 marks)

**Step 3 — Derive the Fisher Criterion:**

The between-class and within-class scatters in the projected 1-D space are:

$$
\tilde{S}_B = \mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}, \quad \tilde{S}_W = \mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}
$$

Maximize the Rayleigh quotient:

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}}
$$

Setting $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 0$ leads to:

$$
\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 4 — Constraint on Number of Directions:**

$\mathbf{S}_B$ is a sum of $C$ rank-1 outer products, but with the constraint $\sum_c N_c (\boldsymbol{\mu}_c - \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \mathbf{0}$, its rank is at most $C - 1$. Hence LDA produces at most $C - 1$ non-zero eigenvalues and projection directions. (1.5 marks)

**Step 5 — Diagram:** A neat sketch of $C$ clusters in original space and their well-separated projections in the LDA-transformed 1-D space. (1 mark)

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Compute Class Means and Global Mean:**

Class 1 mean: $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = \left(\frac{1+2+3}{3}, \frac{2+3+2}{3}\right) = (2, 7/3)$

Class 2 mean: $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = \left(\frac{6+7+5}{3}, \frac{5+6+7}{3}\right) = (6, 6)$

Global mean: $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \left(\frac{19}{6}, \frac{25}{6}\right)$

(1.5 marks)

**Step 2 — Compute Within-Class Scatter Matrix $\mathbf{S}_W$:**

For Class 1:
- $\mathbf{x}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = (-1, 2 - 7/3) = (-1, -1/3)$
- $\mathbf{x}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (0, 3 - 7/3) = (0, 2/3)$
- $\mathbf{x}_3 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_3 = (1, 2 - 7/3) = (1, -1/3)$

Outer products for Class 1:
- $(-1, -1/3)(-1, -1/3)^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1/3 \\ 1/3 & 1/9 \end{bmatrix}$
- $(0, 2/3)(0, 2/3)^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 4/9 \end{bmatrix}$
- $(1, -1/3)(1, -1/3)^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1/3 \\ -1/3 & 1/9 \end{bmatrix}$

Sum for Class 1: $\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 6/9 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2/3 \end{bmatrix}$

For Class 2:
- $\mathbf{x}_4 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (0, 5 - 6) = (0, -1)$
- $\mathbf{x}_5 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (1, 0)$
- $\mathbf{x}_6 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (-1, 1)$

Outer products:
- $(0, -1)(0, -1)^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
- $(1, 0)(1, 0)^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$
- $(-1, 1)(-1, 1)^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$

Sum for Class 2: $\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$

Total $\mathbf{S}_W$:

$$
\mathbf{S}_W = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2/3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & -1 \\ -1 & 8/3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

(2.5 marks)

**Step 3 — Compute Between-Class Scatter Matrix $\mathbf{S}_B$:**

$\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu} = (2 - 19/6, 7/3 - 25/6) = (-7/6, -11/6)$
$\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu} = (6 - 19/6, 6 - 25/6) = (17/6, 11/6)$

Contribution from Class 1 ($N_1 = 3$):

$$
3 \begin{bmatrix} -7/6 \\ -11/6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -7/6 & -11/6 \end{bmatrix} = 3 \begin{bmatrix} 49/36 & 77/36 \\ 77/36 & 121/36 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 49/12 & 77/12 \\ 77/12 & 121/12 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Contribution from Class 2 ($N_2 = 3$):

$$
3 \begin{bmatrix} 17/6 \\ 11/6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 17/6 & 11/6 \end{bmatrix} = 3 \begin{bmatrix} 289/36 & 187/36 \\ 187/36 & 121/36 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 289/12 & 187/12 \\ 187/12 & 121/12 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Total $\mathbf{S}_B$:

$$
\mathbf{S}_B = \begin{bmatrix} 338/12 & 264/12 \\ 264/12 & 242/12 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{12}\begin{bmatrix} 338 & 264 \\ 264 & 242 \end{bmatrix}
$$

(1.5 marks)

**Step 4 — Compute Fisher Direction $\mathbf{w}$:**

For two classes, the closed-form Fisher direction is:

$$
\mathbf{w} \propto \mathbf{S}_W^{-1} (\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)
$$

$\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (2 - 6, 7/3 - 6) = (-4, -11/3)$

$\det(\mathbf{S}_W) = 4 \cdot 8/3 - (-1)(-1) = 32/3 - 1 = 29/3$

$\mathbf{S}_W^{-1} = \frac{3}{29}\begin{bmatrix} 8/3 & 1 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{29}\begin{bmatrix} 8 & 3 \\ 3 & 12 \end{bmatrix}$

$\mathbf{w} \propto \frac{1}{29}\begin{bmatrix} 8 & 3 \\ 3 & 12 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} -4 \\ -11/3 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{29}\begin{bmatrix} -32 - 11 \\ -12 - 44 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{29}\begin{bmatrix} -43 \\ -56 \end{bmatrix}$

Normalize: $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = \frac{1}{29}\sqrt{43^{2} + 56^{2}} = \frac{1}{29}\sqrt{1849 + 3136} = \frac{1}{29}\sqrt{4985} \approx \frac{70.6}{29} \approx 2.435$

So $\mathbf{w} \approx \frac{1}{70.6}\begin{bmatrix} -43 \\ -56 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} -0.609 \\ -0.793 \end{bmatrix}$

(1.5 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Dimensionality Reduction**
> 1. **Forgetting to center the data** before computing the covariance matrix in PCA — this introduces a bias and loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $\mathbf{S}_W$ and $\mathbf{S}_B$** in LDA — within-class scatter is *sum* of class covariances, not the *difference* between class means.
> 3. **Forgetting the normalization** of eigenvectors in PCA — the projection must use unit vectors, otherwise projected coordinates are scaled incorrectly.
> 4. **Missing the $C-1$ limit** for LDA components — students often claim LDA can produce $D$ components, which is wrong.
> 5. **Using raw data without labels** for LDA — LDA is supervised; without labels, the between-class scatter is undefined.
> 6. **In multi-class LDA**, students often forget that the projection dimension is bounded by $\min(C-1, D)$ — both constraints apply.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Dimensionality Reduction** is essential in any pattern recognition system where $D$ is large compared to $N$, to combat the *Curse of Dimensionality*.
- **PCA** is *unsupervised*; it finds directions of *maximum variance*; it diagonalizes the covariance matrix via eigendecomposition.
- **LDA** is *supervised*; it finds directions of *maximum class separability*; it solves a *generalized* eigenproblem $\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}$.
- The **Fisher criterion** $J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}}$ is maximized by LDA.
- PCA can produce up to $\min(N-1, D)$ components; LDA can produce at most $C-1$ components.
- Always **center the data** before PCA (subtract mean). For LDA, in addition to centering, you must compute per-class statistics.
- The proportion of variance retained by keeping the top-$d$ principal components is $\eta = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{D} \lambda_i}$.
- The two-class Fisher direction has a closed-form solution: $\mathbf{w} \propto \mathbf{S}_W^{-1} (\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)$.
- When $\mathbf{S}_W$ is singular (small sample size, $N < D$), use **regularized LDA** with $\mathbf{S}_W + \epsilon \mathbf{I}$ or apply PCA first as a preprocessing step (**PCA+LDA**, used in face recognition).
- PCA's optimality is in **MSE reconstruction**; LDA's optimality is in **class separability (Bayes error approximation under Gaussian assumption)**.
- For KTU 14-mark numericals, expect either: (a) compute covariance and eigenpairs, or (b) compute $\mathbf{S}_W$, $\mathbf{S}_B$ and derive Fisher direction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Text Classification using SVM and Neural Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT415 - FOUNDATIONS OF PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Text Classification using SVM and Neural Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Text Classification?

**Text Classification** is a fundamental supervised learning task in Pattern Recognition and Natural Language Processing (NLP) where a system automatically assigns predefined categorical labels to free-text documents. Formally, given a training set $D = \{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \dots, (x_N, y_N)\}$ where $x_i \in \mathcal{X}$ is a document represented in a feature space and $y_i \in \mathcal{Y} = \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$ is its class label, the goal is to learn a mapping function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ that generalizes to unseen documents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Text classification is a *discriminative pattern recognition* problem where documents are treated as high-dimensional sparse vectors and decision boundaries are learned to maximize class separation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Post Office Mailroom

Imagine a busy post office where thousands of letters arrive daily, and a sorter must drop each letter into one of several pigeonholes: *Bills*, *Personal*, *Spam*, *Promotions*. The sorter does not "read" every word carefully. Instead, the sorter has learned **salient cues**:
- Words like *"free," "offer," "click here"* → likely *Spam*
- Words like *"regards," "meeting," "dear"* → likely *Personal*

A **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** acts like a sorter who draws the *tightest possible line* between piles of letters — a hyperplane in feature space. A **Neural Network** acts like a sorter who has built an internal *hierarchy of cues*: first spotting individual keywords, then word-combinations, then sentence-level patterns, and finally the document's overall topic.

| Term in Analogy | Formal ML Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Letter | Document $x_i$ |
| Pigeonhole | Class label $y_i$ |
| Salient cue | Feature (word, n-gram, embedding) |
| Tightest line | Maximum-margin hyperplane (SVM) |
| Hierarchical learning | Multi-layer neural representation |

## 1.3 Role of SVM and Neural Networks in Text Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these two models?** KTU 2024 emphasizes SVM and Neural Networks because they represent two *paradigmatic extremes* of pattern recognition: SVM is a **discriminative, margin-based, geometric** model, whereas Neural Networks are **function-approximation, gradient-based, distributed representation** models. Mastering both provides a complete view of modern text classification.

### 1.3.1 Support Vector Machines (SVM)
A **binary SVM** finds the hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ that maximizes the *geometric margin* $\frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ between the two classes. For multi-class text classification, strategies include *one-vs-rest (OvR)* and *one-vs-one (OvO)*.

### 1.3.2 Neural Networks
A **feedforward neural network** (also called Multi-Layer Perceptron, MLP) is a composition of linear transformations and non-linear activation functions:
$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \sigma_L \circ (W_L \, \sigma_{L-1} \circ (W_{L-1} \cdots \sigma_1(W_1 \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_1) \cdots ) + \mathbf{b}_{L-1}) + \mathbf{b}_L)$$
For text, modern variants include **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)** for local n-gram patterns and **Recurrent/Transformer-based networks** for sequential context.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Checklist

- **Corpus**: The complete collection of documents used for training/testing.
- **Vocabulary ($V$)**: Set of unique terms (words/tokens) in the corpus.
- **Document-Term Matrix**: A $|D| \times |V|$ matrix encoding term frequencies.
- **Tokenization**: Splitting raw text into discrete units (words, sub-words, characters).
- **Stop Words**: Common words (e.g., "the", "is") often removed to reduce noise.
- **Stemming / Lemmatization**: Reducing words to root form (e.g., "running" → "run").
- **N-gram**: A contiguous sequence of $N$ tokens capturing local context.
- **Word Embedding**: A dense, low-dimensional vector representation of words.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Text Classification Pipeline

The pipeline transforms raw text into a classifier output in four logical stages:

### Stage 1 — Preprocessing
- **Tokenization**, lowercasing, punctuation stripping.
- **Stop-word removal** to reduce dimensionality.
- **Stemming** (rule-based, e.g., Porter) or **Lemmatization** (dictionary-based).
- **Normalization** of unicode and special characters.

### Stage 2 — Feature Extraction (Vectorization)
The two dominant paradigms in KTU 2024 syllabus are:

**(a) Bag-of-Words (BoW) / Term Frequency**
Each document $d$ is represented as a vector $\mathbf{x}_d \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert}$ where the $i$-th entry is the raw count of term $t_i$:
$$x_{d,i} = \text{count}(t_i, d)$$

**(b) TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency)**
This weighs a term by its *discriminative power* across the corpus:
$$\text{tf}(t, d) = \frac{\text{count}(t, d)}{\sum_{t' \in d} \text{count}(t', d)}$$
$$\text{idf}(t) = \log \frac{N}{\text{df}(t) + 1}$$
$$\text{tf-idf}(t, d) = \text{tf}(t, d) \times \text{idf}(t)$$
where $N$ is the total number of documents and $\text{df}(t)$ is the number of documents containing term $t$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why IDF?** A word like "the" appears in almost every document; $\text{idf}$ will be near 0, down-weighting it. A rare but informative word like "photosynthesis" gets a high idf, amplifying its signal.

### Stage 3 — Model Training
- SVM: Solve the convex quadratic optimization for the maximum-margin hyperplane.
- Neural Network: Minimize a loss function (e.g., cross-entropy) using **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** or its variants (Adam, RMSProp).

### Stage 4 — Inference & Evaluation
- Predict $\hat{y} = \arg\max_k P(y=k \mid \mathbf{x})$.
- Evaluate using **Accuracy**, **Precision**, **Recall**, **F1-Score**, and **Confusion Matrix**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory for KTU Board Exams — Memorize this table.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Term Frequency | $\text{tf}(t,d) = \frac{n_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} n_{t',d}}$ | Normalized count of $t$ in $d$ |
| 2 | Inverse Document Frequency | $\text{idf}(t) = \log \frac{N}{\text{df}(t)+1}$ | Penalizes corpus-common terms |
| 3 | TF-IDF Weight | $w_{t,d} = \text{tf}(t,d) \cdot \text{idf}(t)$ | Final feature value |
| 4 | SVM Hyperplane | $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | Decision boundary |
| 5 | Geometric Margin | $\gamma = \frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Distance of closest point to hyperplane |
| 6 | SVM Primal (hard) | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \;\; \text{s.t.} \; y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1$ | Linearly separable case |
| 7 | SVM Primal (soft) | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_i \xi_i \;\; \text{s.t.} \; y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1-\xi_i$ | Introduces slack $\xi_i \ge 0$ |
| 8 | Hinge Loss | $\ell_i = \max(0,\, 1 - y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b))$ | Surrogate for 0–1 loss |
| 9 | Kernel Trick | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$ | Implicit non-linear mapping |
| 10 | RBF Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Most common non-linear kernel |
| 11 | Softmax | $\sigma(\mathbf{z})_k = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$ | Multi-class output activation |
| 12 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{i,k} \log \hat{y}_{i,k}$ | NN classification loss |
| 13 | Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Binary output activation |
| 14 | ReLU | $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$ | Hidden-layer activation |
| 15 | Gradient Descent Update | $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \, \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$ | NN parameter update |
| 16 | Word2Vec (Skip-gram) Objective | $\max \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{-c \le j \le c,\, j \ne 0} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t)$ | Embedding learning |
| 17 | Softmax (Skip-gram) | $P(w_O \mid w_I) = \frac{\exp(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^\top \mathbf{v}_{w_I})}{\sum_{w=1}^{V} \exp(\mathbf{v}_w^\top \mathbf{v}_{w_I})}$ | Context word probability |
| 18 | Cosine Similarity | $\text{sim}(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = \frac{\mathbf{u}^\top \mathbf{v}}{\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert \, \Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert}$ | Comparing embeddings |
| 19 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R |
| 20 | One-vs-Rest Decision | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} (\mathbf{w}_k^\top \mathbf{x} + b_k)$ | Multi-class SVM inference |

## 2.3 Why and How Behind Each Step — The 'Why' Layer

- **Why normalize tf?** Raw counts bias toward long documents. Normalization $\text{tf}$ gives equal footing to short and long documents.
- **Why add $1$ in the IDF denominator?** To avoid division by zero when $\text{df}(t) = N$ (term appears in every doc), and to bound the weight.
- **Why $C$ in soft-margin SVM?** $C$ balances *margin width* vs. *misclassification tolerance*. Large $C$ → hard margin, small $C$ → wider margin, more violations.
- **Why ReLU in hidden layers?** ReLU mitigates the **vanishing gradient problem** of sigmoid/tanh, enabling deeper networks.
- **Why cosine similarity for embeddings?** Word vectors live on a hypersphere; cosine measures *angular* proximity, not magnitude, which is what semantic similarity demands.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Feature | Model |
|---|---|---|
| Email Spam Detection | TF-IDF on words | Linear SVM (industry standard) |
| Sentiment Analysis (Reviews) | Word embeddings + CNN | CNN/LSTM |
| News Topic Categorization | TF-IDF on n-grams | Linear SVM |
| Legal Document Classification | Sentence embeddings (BERT) | Fine-tuned Transformer |
| Hate-Speech / Toxic Comment Detection | Sub-word tokens (FastText) | Shallow CNN |
| Medical Record ICD Coding | TF-IDF + handcrafted features | Linear SVM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Despite the deep-learning revolution, *linear SVM on TF-IDF remains a strong, fast, and interpretable baseline* for many KTU-level and industrial text classification tasks — a fact often tested in board exams.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Soft-Margin SVM Objective from First Principles

We start from the geometric intuition that the *best* hyperplane maximizes the margin between two classes.

**Step 1:** Let the hyperplane be $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$. The perpendicular (signed) distance from a point $\mathbf{x}_i$ to this hyperplane is:
$$d_i = \frac{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

**Step 2:** For a *correctly classified* point of class $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, we require:
$$y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) > 0$$

**Step 3:** The margin is the *minimum* distance of any training point to the hyperplane. For class $+1$ support vector, we scale $\mathbf{w}, b$ such that $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_+ + b = +1$; for class $-1$ support vector, $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_- + b = -1$. Subtracting:
$$\mathbf{w}^\top (\mathbf{x}_+ - \mathbf{x}_-) = 2$$
Dividing by $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$, the geometric margin is $\frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$.

**Step 4:** Maximizing the margin $\frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ is equivalent to minimizing $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ subject to constraints:
$$\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \quad & \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1, \quad \forall i \in \{1, \dots, N\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Real-world text data is *rarely* linearly separable. Introduce slack variables $\xi_i \ge 0$ that allow margin violations, and a penalty parameter $C$ to control the trade-off:
$$\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \quad & \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \ge 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** The equivalent unconstrained (regularized loss) form, which is what SGD optimizers actually minimize, is:
$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b) = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \max(0,\, 1 - y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b))$$
where the second term is the **hinge loss** summed over all training points. The parameter $C$ plays the role of $\frac{1}{\lambda}$ in standard $L_2$-regularized loss formulations.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gradient of the Hinge Loss for SGD Training

Linear SVMs on text are often trained with SGD because $|V|$ is too large for quadratic programming.

**Step 1:** Define the per-sample loss:
$$\ell_i = \max(0,\, 1 - y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b))$$

**Step 2:** The indicator function $I(y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) < 1)$ captures whether the sample is *inside the margin or misclassified*:
$$\ell_i = \bigl(1 - y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b)\bigr) \cdot I\bigl(y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) < 1\bigr)$$

**Step 3:** Compute the gradient with respect to $\mathbf{w}$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \ell_i}{\partial \mathbf{w}} &= -y_i \mathbf{x}_i \cdot I\bigl(y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) < 1\bigr)
\end{aligned}$$
For $\mathbf{w}$ this is $-y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ if the sample violates the margin, and $0$ otherwise.

**Step 4:** Compute the gradient with respect to $b$:
$$\frac{\partial \ell_i}{\partial b} = -y_i \cdot I\bigl(y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) < 1\bigr)$$

**Step 5:** Add the $L_2$-regularization term $\frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ (its gradient is simply $\mathbf{w}$). The full SGD update becomes:
$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{w} &\leftarrow \mathbf{w} - \eta \bigl( \mathbf{w} - C \cdot y_i \mathbf{x}_i \cdot I(\text{margin violated}) \bigr) \\
b &\leftarrow b + \eta \cdot C \cdot y_i \cdot I(\text{margin violated})
\end{aligned}$$
**Interpretation:** For *correctly classified points outside the margin*, only regularization shrinks $\mathbf{w}$. For *violators*, the update pushes $\mathbf{w}$ in the direction that correctly classifies the point.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Softmax + Cross-Entropy Gradient (for Neural Networks)

For a multi-class text classifier with $K$ classes, the output layer is:
$$\mathbf{z} = W^{(L)} \mathbf{h}^{(L-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(L)}, \qquad \hat{\mathbf{y}} = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{z})$$

**Step 1:** The cross-entropy loss for a single sample with one-hot label $\mathbf{y}$ is:
$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \hat{y}_k$$

**Step 2:** The softmax function is:
$$\hat{y}_k = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k}$. Using the chain rule and the fact that $\frac{\partial \hat{y}_j}{\partial z_k} = \hat{y}_k (\delta_{jk} - \hat{y}_j)$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k} &= -\sum_{j=1}^{K} y_j \cdot \frac{1}{\hat{y}_j} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_j}{\partial z_k} \\
&= -\sum_{j=1}^{K} y_j \cdot \frac{1}{\hat{y}_j} \cdot \hat{y}_k(\delta_{jk} - \hat{y}_j) \\
&= -\sum_{j=1}^{K} y_j (\delta_{jk} - \hat{y}_j) \\
&= -\bigl(y_k - \hat{y}_k \sum_{j=1}^{K} y_j \bigr) \\
&= \hat{y}_k - y_k \quad \text{(since } \sum_j y_j = 1 \text{ for one-hot labels)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** The gradient with respect to the weight matrix $W^{(L)}$ (with $\mathbf{h}^{(L-1)}$ the previous-layer activation) is:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(L)}} = (\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}) \, \mathbf{h}^{(L-1)\top}$$

**Step 5:** This elegant result $(\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y})$ is one of the most important in deep learning — the gradient of softmax-cross-entropy is simply the **prediction error**! It propagates backward to earlier layers via the chain rule of backpropagation.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Word2Vec Skip-gram Softmax and Negative Sampling Approximation

**Step 1:** The full softmax over vocabulary $V$ (often $|V| > 10^5$) is computationally prohibitive:
$$P(w_O \mid w_I) = \frac{\exp(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^\top \mathbf{v}_{w_I})}{\sum_{w=1}^{V} \exp(\mathbf{v}_w^\top \mathbf{v}_{w_I})}$$

**Step 2:** Replace the expensive denominator with **negative sampling**. Draw $K$ "noise" words $w_i \sim P_n(w)$ from a unigram distribution raised to the $3/4$ power. The new objective becomes:
$$\mathcal{L} = \log \sigma(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^\top \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) + \sum_{i=1}^{K} \mathbb{E}_{w_i \sim P_n} \bigl[ \log \sigma(-\mathbf{v}_{w_i}^\top \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) \bigr]$$

**Step 3:** Update rule via SGD, where $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ and $\sigma(-x) = 1 - \sigma(x)$. For a positive sample $(w_I, w_O)$:
$$\mathbf{v}_{w_I} \leftarrow \mathbf{v}_{w_I} + \eta \bigl[ \sigma(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^\top \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) - 1 \bigr] \mathbf{v}_{w_O}$$
$$\mathbf{v}_{w_O} \leftarrow \mathbf{v}_{w_O} + \eta \bigl[ \sigma(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^\top \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) - 1 \bigr] \mathbf{v}_{w_I}$$
For each negative sample, the sign flips. This yields dense embeddings of dimension 50–300 that capture semantic similarity (e.g., $\mathbf{v}_{\text{king}} - \mathbf{v}_{\text{man}} + \mathbf{v}_{\text{woman}} \approx \mathbf{v}_{\text{queen}}$).

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Aspect | SVM | Neural Network |
|---|---|---|
| **Decision Boundary** | Maximum-margin hyperplane | Non-linear, hierarchical |
| **Loss Function** | Hinge loss | Cross-entropy / MSE |
| **Optimization** | QP (or SGD for large data) | SGD / Adam / RMSProp |
| **Feature Input** | TF-IDF, BoW | Embeddings, TF-IDF |
| **Capacity Control** | $C$, kernel, $\sigma$ | Depth, width, dropout, weight decay |
| **Interpretability** | High (top-weighted features) | Low to medium |
| **Scalability to Large Data** | Linear SVM scales well | NN scales well with GPUs |
| **Performance on Sparse Data** | Excellent (text) | Needs embeddings for best results |
| **Handles Non-linearity** | Via kernel trick | Natively, through activations |
| **Training Time** | Moderate to slow (kernel) | Fast per epoch (linear models) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Text Classification End-to-End Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Corpus] --> B[Tokenization and Lowercasing]
    B --> C[Stopword Removal and Stemming]
    C --> D[Vocabulary Construction]
    D --> E[Feature Extraction: TF-IDF or Embeddings]
    E --> F{Select Model}
    F --> G[Linear SVM Classifier]
    F --> H[Feedforward Neural Network]
    G --> I[Predicted Class Label]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Evaluation: Accuracy, F1, Confusion Matrix]
```

## 4.2 SVM Decision Boundary for Binary Text Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Positive Class: Class plus 1] --> M
    N1[Negative Class: Class minus 1] --> M
    M[Maximum Margin Hyperplane: w transpose x plus b equals 0] --> SV
    SV[Support Vectors: Points on Margin Boundary] --> Out[Decision Function: sign of w transpose x plus b]
    Out --> Class1[Label: plus 1]
    Out --> Class2[Label: minus 1]
```

## 4.3 Neural Network Architecture for Text Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Inp[Input Text: Sequence of Token IDs] --> Emb[Embedding Layer: size V x d]
    Emb --> P1[Average or Max Pool Over Tokens]
    P1 --> H1[Hidden Layer 1: ReLU Activation, size 128]
    H1 --> H2[Hidden Layer 2: ReLU Activation, size 64]
    H2 --> Drop[Dropout Layer: rate equals 0.5]
    Drop --> Out[Output Layer: size K, Softmax Activation]
    Out --> Pred[Predicted Probability Distribution over K Classes]
```

## 4.4 SVM Optimization Flow (Primal-Dual Perspective)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Training Data xi, yi] --> P[Primal: min half w squared plus C sum xi]
    P --> Lag[Construct Lagrangian with multipliers alpha and mu]
    Lag --> KKT[Apply KKT Conditions]
    KKT --> Dual[Dual: max sum alpha minus half sum alpha i alpha j yi yj K xi xj]
    Dual --> SMO[Sequential Minimal Optimization or SGD Solver]
    SMO --> Soln[Recover w and b from alpha]
    Soln --> Pred[Inference via sign of w transpose x plus b]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hybrid TF-IDF + Embedding Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Raw[Raw Document] --> Tok[Tokenizer Module]
    Tok --> TFIDF[TF-IDF Vectorizer: Sparse High-Dim Output]
    Tok --> Emb[Embedding Lookup: Dense Low-Dim Output]
    TFIDF --> Concat[Feature Concatenation Layer]
    Emb --> Concat
    Concat --> Cls[Classifier: SVM or MLP]
    Cls --> Label[Final Class Label]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap
